coredata.dc:title: Frontiers in polymer/carbon dots nanocomposites: Recent advances in synthesis and diverse applications

coredata.dc:description: A novel category of zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon nanomaterials measuring less than 10 nm in size known as carbon dots (CDs) has become an emerging frontier in material science. Due to the size of quantum dots, the presence of oxygen functional groups, and excellent fluorescence properties, CDs grabbed the attention of researchers towards the preparation of polymer nanocomposites. Due to their diverse properties, including functionality, aqueous dispersibility, ease of preparation from various biosources, and biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) hold significant potential for applications in biosensors, energy, optoelectronics, and sensors. When CDs are integrated into polymer matrices, these properties are enhanced, making high-performance polymer/CD nanocomposites. The synergistic interaction between the polymer matrix and CDs results in enhanced mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties. Polymers provide structural integrity, flexibility, and processability, while CDs contribute to the nanocomposite's optical activity, electrical conductivity, and environmental stability. Moreover, the surface functionalities of CDs can be tailored to improve compatibility with various polymers, enabling the design of targeted and customisable products. There are many review articles that presented the polymer/CDs nanocomposites with a narrow focus on very limited applications and properties. This review describes the synthesis approaches of Polymer/CDs nanocomposites, highlighting their diverse properties and applications in environmental, energy storage, and biomedical sectors. The review output will serve as a repository, which will facilitate further study and advancement in the field of polymer/CDs nanocomposites and their applicability.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel category of zero-dimensional (0-D) carbon nanomaterials measuring less than 10 nm in size known as carbon dots (CDs) has become an emerging frontier in material science. Due to the size of quantum dots, the presence of oxygen functional groups, and excellent fluorescence properties, CDs grabbed the attention of researchers towards the preparation of polymer nanocomposites. Due to their diverse properties, including functionality, aqueous dispersibility, ease of preparation from various biosources, and biocompatibility, carbon dots (CDs) hold significant potential for applications in biosensors, energy, optoelectronics, and sensors. When CDs are integrated into polymer matrices, these properties are enhanced, making high-performance polymer/CD nanocomposites. The synergistic interaction between the polymer matrix and CDs results in enhanced mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties. Polymers provide structural integrity, flexibility, and processability, while CDs contribute to the nanocomposite's optical activity, electrical conductivity, and environmental stability. Moreover, the surface functionalities of CDs can be tailored to improve compatibility with various polymers, enabling the design of targeted and customisable products. There are many review articles that presented the polymer/CDs nanocomposites with a narrow focus on very limited applications and properties. This review describes the synthesis approaches of Polymer/CDs nanocomposites, highlighting their diverse properties and applications in environmental, energy storage, and biomedical sectors. The review output will serve as a repository, which will facilitate further study and advancement in the field of polymer/CDs nanocomposites and their applicability.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocomposites are heterogeneous phase materials with at least one component in the nanometer dimension. Polymer nanocomposites are a class of material comprises discontinuous nanofiller dispersed in a continuous organic polymer phase [1,2]. A homogenous blend of carbon dots (CDs) as reinforcement nanofiller within polymer matrices or the surface of CDs fabricated with rich layer of polymer can be categorized as polymer/CD nanocomposite.

The concept of reinforcement of polymer by integrating nanomaterial was introduced in the late 20th century. Primarily, it was initiated with clay, silica, and metal oxides-based composites formulation. However, during the period of 1990–2000s, the venture for novel nanocomposites using wide varieties of nanoparticles including graphene, carbon nanotubes, quantum dots has been started [3,4]. The integration of carbon dot in polymer matrices found to be reported in early 2010’s aiming to incorporate the unique optical, electrical, and physical properties of CDs in composite form. Several advanced techniques like in-situ polymerization, solution mixing, melt blending as well as surface functionalization of carbon dot were developed and optimized for synthesis of proper well-dispersed polymer carbon dot nanocomposite. The integration of carbon dots into polymer matrices as composites has significantly enhanced their optical, electrical, mechanical, and thermal properties. In recent years, the field of polymer carbon dot composites has experienced significant growth, leading to a wide range of multidimensional applications, including flexible electronics, conductive films, supercapacitors, photodetectors, biomedicines, biosensors, textiles, and antimicrobial agents. Another important aspect of nanocomposite research is the development of sustainable, biocompatible and biodegradable alternative of synthetic polymer.

The discovery of synthetic polymers in the early twentieth century and onwards development of related technology have revolutionized every aspect of modern life and human civilization. Currently, the annual global production of polymer is about 400 million tons which indicates their significant demand for commercial use [5–7]. However, the use of fossil fuel and energy intensive production processes imposes serious environmental concern through the emission of green-house gases, carbon footprint and non-renewable energy resource consumption. The unusual resistance of synthetic polymers towards physical and chemical degradation leads to major environmental pollution, unsustainability, disposal and land filling issue which is a threat for overall ecosystem. According to the recent reports, synthetic polymers contribute approximately 50 % of global waste, and only 20 % of the total polymer waste can be recycled. The concept of biodegradable polymer, with hydrolysable linkages, introduced in the late 1970’s, became the most preferred alternative to synthetic polymers due to their natural process of degradation and least impact on ecosystem. Since 1980’s, significant research effort has been invested for thorough investigation of renewable resources to design polymers of natural benign, or using natural monomeric precursors for polymerization or incorporation of bioactive moieties in the traditional polymers of synthetic origin. However, several polymers like polyesters, polysaccharide, polyurethane, poly (ethylene terephthalate), poly (lactic acid) etc. derived from renewable resources such as plant sugars like starch, cellulose, chitin, chitosan, animal proteins like collagen, gelatin, latex of rubber, lignin, vegetable oils, and terpenes to minimize the usage of fossil fuel. Numerous commercially successful biodegradable polymers including poly(lactic acid), poly(hydroxyalkanoate), poly(glycolic acid), and poly(butyl succinate) [8]. Mechanical and physical properties of bio-based polymers are insufficient for high-end application and not resistant to stringent thermal and chemical process during fabrication. However, in this backdrop, polymer nanocomposites are the most reliable alternative with elementary improvement of each aspect of synthetic or bio-based pristine polymers like mechanical, optical, chemical, thermal and physical properties require for commercial applications. In this context, wide varieties of nanocomposites have been reported with different nanomaterial like carbon dots, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, reduced graphene oxide, titanium oxide, silver, quantum dots etc., with extensive application potential.

One of the most significant achievements of nanoscience is the discovery of carbon-based nanomaterials with remarkable features suitable for broad spectrum application from electronics to medicine. Carbon nanomaterials are a diverse group of materials having various form of carbon that includes carbon nanotube, graphene, carbon dot, fullerene, carbon nanofiber, nanodiamond and other carbon allotropes with nano-dimension. Among all these materials, CDs are the newest addition of carbonaceous family because of quasi-spherical and carbon atoms clustered domains which is of less than 10 nm size. In 2004, Xu et al. first reported CDs, while separating single wall carbon nanotubes (CNT) from carbon soot made by arc discharge using gel electrophoresis [9]. In 2006, Sun et al. reported on fluorescent carbon dots, introducing a novel domain within the discipline of carbon-based nanomaterials, which encompasses carbon nanotubes, fullerenes, nanodiamonds, and graphene [10]. The discovery of carbon dots presents a promising alternative characterized by superior optical properties and excellent biocompatibility. The interaction of quantum size effects, surface defect states, and adjustable band gap in conjugated π-domains leads to programmable, size-dependent fluorescence properties, as well as up-conversion photoluminescence properties and notable photostability [11]. Moreover, the carbon dots small size, large surface area, hydrophilic surface functionalities as well as π-rich core domain allow interaction of carbon dot with versatile polymer, metal as well as biological entities. Additional benefits include a low-cost synthesis route, wide range of easily available versatile precursors, rich surface chemistry, metal free composition, unique optical property and low toxicity. Furthermore, the applicability amplified folds when CDs meet the domain of polymeric macromolecule. Polymer/CD nanocomposites are receiving significant attention owing to their exceptional mechanical, photophysical, electrical, and chemical properties. Due to the reactive surface functionalities, carbon dot considered as an enriched nanofiller component of nanocomposite material. The fabrication of polymer/CD nanocomposite diversified its application potential towards sensing [12], catalysis [13], optoelectronics [14], energy device [15], packaging [16], biomedical [17], therapeutics [18–21], diagnosis [22], and various commercial aspects. The evolution of carbon dot-based materials chemistry can be divided into three phases (Fig. 1 a): (I) the discovery phase (2004–2006), (II) the early development phase (2007–2011), and (III) the rapid expansion phase (2011–present). Phase (I) denotes the initial period during which CDs were first identified and defined. However, Fig. 1b shows the varied range of applications of carbon dot-based materials chemistry during these years [23].

This review article offers a novel perspective by exploring the synergy between two cutting-edge materials: polymers and carbon dots (CDs). This combination represents a transformative approach to creating nanocomposites with enhanced functionalities, bridging the gap between material science, nanotechnology, and practical applications. This review goes beyond traditional discussions by delving into the latest advancements in the synthesis strategies of polymer/CD nanocomposites, emphasizing scalable, eco-friendly, and cost-effective approaches. Additionally, it recognizes emerging applications, including advanced catalysis, multifunctional coatings, and next-generation technologies across the fields of environment, storage of energy, and biomedical devices, while tackling essential challenges such as enhancing interface compatibility and ensuring reproducibility.

The synthesis approaches for polymer/CD nanocomposite can be discussed in two segments, synthesis approaches for carbon dot, polymeric component, and techniques for homogeneous blending of polymer and carbon dot to form nanocomposite. All the available approaches for the synthesis of ultra-low particle size (<10 nm) carbon-dot can broadly categorized in two major ways, entitled as top-down (physical method) and bottom-up (chemical method) [24,25].

The top-down strategy signifies to all synthesis procedures that involve precursors with a larger mass of carbon, such as graphite, graphene, and activated carbon, among others. Popular top-down procedures for the creation of carbon dots include laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation. Top-down CDs often have a flawless Sp2 structure with fewer surface imperfections, resulting in weak fluorescence characteristics. To enhance the quantum yield requires further chemical treatment. In contrast, bottom-up approaches involve the disintegration/breakdown of large molecular precursors followed by the chemical synthesis of colloidal nanosized carbon dots from molecular precursors using thermal, electrical, or chemical energy [26,27]. The popular bottom-up techniques are hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave irradiation, and/or pyrolysis. The considerable advantages of these approaches are low-energy-consuming techniques, simple equipment, and a wide range of low-cost precursors. Bottom-up approaches enable the synthesis of carbon dioxide from a diverse range of natural raw materials, including biomolecules, biomass, waste materials, and several natural products, using non-toxic reagents. Different synthesis approaches are used for combining carbon dots and polymers of interest to form the desired nanocomposite, like solution blending, in-situ polymerization, solution casting, in-situ reverse micro-emulsion polymerization, stirring, drop casting, sol-gel, phase inversion, solvent evaporation, and electrospinning (Table 1 ).

Precursor materials for carbon dot synthesis can be of natural, benign, or anthropogenic (synthetic/semi-synthetic) [24,25]. Use of natural products over synthetic materials as precursor of carbon dot is the newest trend because of its multifaceted advantages [28,29]. The primary requirements for large-scale production are the sufficient availability of natural precursors in the local surroundings and their low cost. Delicate balance of carbon and heteroatoms in the lattice is the key to the versatile optical properties of the carbon dot [24]. Natural precursors, characterized by a high content of heteroatoms and a distinctive carbon-to-heteroatom ratio, inherently introduce surface defects within the carbon dot lattice and contribute a range of hydrophilic functional groups on their surfaces. Additionally, the preparation of carbon dioxide from biological waste can convert or transform low-value material into a value-added functional product. A wide variety of natural precursors in terms of origin/source and chemical composition have been reported [29]. Natural precursors can be broadly classified into a few major groups like small molecules, green biomass, biopolymers, and biowaste [28–31]. However, compared to CDs from synthetic materials, CDs from natural precursors require significant pre-processing before synthesis, lack precise control over chemical composition, are less reproducible, have less tunable optical properties, and have a lower quantum yield.

A broad range of polymers has been reported, including natural polymers like alginate, chitosan, cellulose acetate, starch, collagen, etc., and synthetic polymers like polyvinyl alcohol, (3-aminopropyl) triethoxy silane, polyethyleneimine, polystyrene, polyethersulfone, polypropylene, polyimide, polyacrylonitrile, polyethylene, polyethylene glycol, polyimide, polyaniline, etc. In the case of in-situ polymerisation, several monomeric precursors of polymers such as dopamine, pyrrole, piperazine, acrylamide monomers, aniline, etc. can be employed for different types of polymerisation approaches, such as free radical dispersion polymerisation, cross-linking polymerisation, acid/base-catalysed polymerisation, photo-induced polymerisation, Schiff-base-mediated polymerisation, etc. (Table 1). Synthetic precursors are costly compared to the natural precursors but provide precise control over components, resulting in tunable physical, chemical, and optical properties of composites and reproducibility required for desired fabrication targeted towards application. However, the use of natural precursors and green synthesizers has been emphasized for their eco-friendliness, though the process is empirical and less reproducible.

Hydrothermal treatment is one of the most reported routes for synthesis of carbon dioxide from wide varieties of natural products [24,42]. The precursor biomass is converted to a carbonized structure by thermochemical conversion using high temperatures. The carbonisation process involves a combination of several consecutive chemical changes through hydrolysis, dehydration, condensation, polymerisation, aromatisation, etc. Pure water is the only solvent used here. A precursor material solution is contained within a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to temperatures between 70 and 260 °C for a duration of 24 h in a heating oven, resulting in the auto-generation of high pressure. The solution's colour transitions progressively from yellow to light brown and then to dark brown as carbonization occurs. Finally, segregation and cleansing steps are required to obtain pure CDs. Natural precursors are majorly composed of carbohydrate, protein, and lipid molecules, rich in natural carbon and heterocyclic molecules. In general, complex carbohydrates are converted into simpler monosaccharides by hydrolysis, followed by furfural formation due to dehydration during low-temperature carbonization, which initiates polymerization. Proteins convert into amino acids due to complete hydrolysis. Further condensation and polymerization of such reactive units produces small molecular-weight soluble polymers. At a critical concentration, these reactive polymers are thought to initiate the development of carbon dots through nuclear burst at the supersaturation point. However, the synthesised carbon dot is highly water-soluble due to the presence of oxygen-rich surface functionalities. The unique advantages of this technique include its low energy input, the use of water as both a solvent and a reactant, and its non-toxicity, affordability, and abundance.

Solvothermal treatment can be considered an alternative to the hydrothermal route, where organic solvents are used instead of water, which yields hydrophobic carbon dots. This is a facile approach for synthesizing carbon dots from wide varieties of amphiphilic to non-polar precursors with relatively high carbonization temperatures. In general, precursors along with proper capping agents solubilised in compatible non-aqueous solvents are subjected to carbonisation using high temperature (100–1000 °C) and vapour pressure in a sealed teflon-coated stainless-steel autoclave. The choice of precursor and solvent, precursor to capping agent ratio, is a determinant of size, chemical structure, surface functionalities, and defect sites. Reaction temperature and duration influence directly the nucleation, growth kinetics, and crystallinity of as-synthesised carbon dots. Quantum yield and emission wavelength are functions of size, sp2 to sp3 carbon ratio, surface defect, and lattice structure. The solvothermal route provides precise control over particle size, tunable photoluminescence property, and higher quantum yield. However, hydrophobic carbon dots synthesized via the solvothermal route can be converted to hydrophilic through further surface functionalization [32]. Use of toxic organic solvents and high temperature requirements are considered potential disadvantages of solvothermal procedures.

Pyrolysis is another efficient, simple, cost-effective, and versatile approach for carbon dioxide synthesis. This method entails the thermal decomposition of organic precursors at elevated temperatures in an oxygen-free environment, resulting in the synthesis of carbon dots. A variety of carbon-rich small molecules are used as carbon precursors, like carbohydrates, citric acid, etc. In addition, different nitrogen-rich molecules like urea can be mixed with carbon precursors as dopant sources. The mixture is placed in a ceramic crucible or microwave-safe container and allowed to heat in a furnace or microwave at an elevated temperature (200–300 °C) for a specified time in an inert atmosphere. The resultant desired carbon dot can be purified by gel filtration chromatography, dialysis, etc. Microwave-assisted formulation of carbon dots from small molecular precursors is efficient, tunable, ecofriendly, and economic [24,42]. The advantages include short reaction times due to rapid uniform heating, which leads to improved yield, monodispersed, and purity. Usually, the precursors are allowed to disperse in a proper solvent, and the content is heated in microwave equipment for a brief time duration, resultant CDs are segregated and purified.

The chemical oxidation of carbon-rich precursors to synthesize carbon dioxide is a widely reported method. This method provides technical simplicity and enables the introduction of various reactive functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, thereby improving solubility and reactivity for future applications. This approach typically involves the oxidation of carbon-rich precursors using strong oxidising agents. Graphite powder, carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbohydrates, and other carbon-rich precursors are frequently utilized precursors that experience significant oxidation through a combination of strong acids (nitric acid, sulfuric acid) under reflux conditions at moderately elevated temperatures (100–150 °C) for an extended duration (12–24 h). This step oxidizes the precursor material and converts it to carbon dioxide. The recovery of the desired carbon dot includes neutralization with dilute sodium hydroxide or ammonia, purification, and drying.

Yang and colleagues fabricated a polyvinyl alcohol/carbon dot (PVA/CD) nanocomposite using a microwave and a blending approach, resulting in a tunable pH-responsive fluorescence-labelled water-induced shape memory polymer (SMP) [32]. The luminescent yellow emissive CD was prepared by a microwave-hydrothermal approach. Microwave-assisted synthesis facilitates a shorter reaction time and maximizes the yield of CD by minimizing the possibility of secondary reactions. Equimolar (2 mmol) dilute acidic solution of resorcinol and o-phenylenediamine was microwaved at 180 °C for 10 min. The resultant crystalline CD (6.1±1.2 nm average size) has well-formed lattice fringes of 0.21 nm lattice spacing close to d-spacing of grapheme planes with strong yellow emission (QY 62.8 %) (Fig. 2 ). The CD (25 mg) was homogenized with a pre-prepared PVA solution (2 g) for preparation of the nanocomposite. The incorporation of a luminescent carbon dot in the PVA matrix provided superior shape recovery performance actuated by water, pH responsiveness, glow in the dark, and better mechanical properties compared to the PVA-based SMP.

Microwave-induced polymerization of the precursors produced a Sp2-hybridized carbon cluster, along with numerous active aromatic intermediate fragments and carbon vacancies. Further intermolecular dehydration of such active fragments catalysed by HCl facilitates the formation of carbon cores accompanied by complete carbonization. Finally, when a carbon core with a large extended conjugated sp2 domain and doped N of pyrrolic nature is present in the conjugated system, it can produce yellow emissive CDs. The presence of pyrrolic N populates the electron density in the system, facilitating radiative recombination and imparting a significantly high quantum yield. Based on their detailed structural analysis, the introduction of fluorescent carbon dots as nanofillers in the PVA matrix significantly improved the shape memory performance of the matrix due to the formation of an optimal number of additional cross-link points in the matrix. Moreover, the fluorescence property of the CD in the nanocomposite offers additional tracking features for the recovery process in the dark. Zhang et al. described an easy method for the one-pot synthesis of a polysiloxane-terminated fluorescent carbon dot nanocomposite under mild conditions. This material serves as a coating for solar cells, enhancing power conversion efficiency, and is produced via a solvothermal in-situ polymer growth technique [33]. L-Ascorbic acid, the carbon precursor, was dissolved in a water-ethanol (1:1, v/v) solvent system along with the polymer precursor N-(2-aminoethyl)-3-aminopropyl)tris-(2-ethoxy) silane (KH791) and reacted at 92 °C for 12 h, resulting in silane-modified fluorescent carbon dots. Further drying at 70 °C for removal of solvent allowed the hydrolytic condensation of silane monomer, resulting in polysiloxane-CD nanocomposite. The resulting polymer/CD nanocomposite exhibits exceptionally stable PL properties in the solid-state and demonstrates PL down-shifting phenomena. A single-layer nanocomposite coating on the cover glass of P3HT: PCBM-based BHJ solar cell surfaces has demonstrated a 12 % improvement in power conversion efficiency. The KH791 serves as a polymerizable monomer alongside a surface passivation agent, enhancing the photoluminescence intensity of the carbon dots. The presence of KH791 effectively inhibited agglomeration in the solid state and the associated quenching of photoluminescence in the solid state. The proposed synthesis mechanism involves sequential reduction, hydrolysis, polymerization, and carbonization. The aqueous solution of ascorbic acid promotes its oxidation to dehydroascorbate, which further hydrolyses to 2-ketogluconic acid or 2, 3-ketogluconic acid. Alkaline KH791 acts as a catalyst for the oxidation of ascorbate to dehydroascorbate. The 2-ketogluconic acid acts as a precursor of polymerisation through intermolecular dehydration or aldol condensation and produces the polymeric CD at a low carbonisation temperature (<80 °C). Further carbonisation of polymeric CDs (p-CDs) to carbogenic CDs (c-CDs) via intramolecular dehydration or ketoenol tautomerism catalysed by KH791 at moderately high temperature (92 °C) results in CDs. The formation of a nanocomposite polysiloxane encapsulated cyclodextrin (CD) results from a linkage between the silicon of KH791 and the surface oxygen of CD, established during the drying process at 72 °C through hydrolytic condensation [33].

Imae T and Devadas B utilized a hydrothermal method, followed by a surfactant-assisted in-situ oxidative polymerization technique, to synthesize conducting polymer/CD nanocomposites, such as PPy@CD and PANI@CD [34]. The incorporation of CD into the polymer as a nanocomposite significantly enhances the electrical conductivity and specific capacitance of both PPy and PANI. The composition of CDs in polymers influences the optimal capacitance performance of the designed pseudocapacitor. Carbon dioxide was initially prepared from an aqueous solution containing a molar equivalent of citric acid and ethylenediamine in a 1:1 molar ratio (COOH: NH2) within an autoclave at 230 °C for a duration of 4 h. In the subsequent step, we prepared a nanocomposite of conducting polymers (PPy or PANI) and CDs using the cationic surfactants cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and ammonium persulfate (APS) at three different molar ratios of monomer and raw materials of CDs: 1:0.4, 1:1, and 1:2. Typically, 0.01 M CTAB in 0.1 M HCl was added to different volumes of CDs, followed by the addition of 0.025 M pyrrole or aniline at 0–5 °C with stirring for 30 min. Then in-situ polymerisation was initiated by addition of 0.025 M APS for 1 day at 0–5 °C. The product was subjected to several cleaning cycles with water and ethanol, and subsequently dried at 50 °C for 12 h. The interaction between polymers and cyclodextrin (CD) in the composite is demonstrated by the shifts in the C=C (1550 cm-1) and C-N stretching vibration bands of pyrrole in the PPy@CD composite, as well as the shift in the C=C (1550 cm-1) band of polyaniline (PANI). The Sp2 hybridized core of CDs acts as a intersection between conducting polymer matrix, which facilitates electron transfer and reflects the higher capacitance performance. Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) is of high demand surface coating polymer because of its versatility, low-cost easy processing, and eco-friendliness. In contrast, WPU suffers from few serious shortcomings regarding its inferior mechanical strength, poor chemical resistance, and thermal stability. It is reported that modifications like blending, incorporation of nanofillers can significantly elevates the material properties of WPUs. Most of the nanofillers suffer from agglomeration due to lack of required functionality and chemical inertness. The enhancement of chemical and physical properties in WPU-based materials is primarily dependent on homogeneous distribution, stable chemical interactions, and optimized dispersion of nanofillers within the polymer matrix. In this context, Karak et al. [35] have fabricated eco-friendly, low VOC content thermoset waterborne polyurethane/CD nanocomposite (WPU/CD) with improved mechanical, (4.to 8.5 MPa tensile strength), thermal stability (from 250 to 280 °C) compared to pristine thermosetting WPU via solvothermal followed by in-situ polymerization approach. The functional CD was synthesized from pulp-free banana juice using ethanol, subjected to heating at 150 °C in a glass reactor for a duration of 2.5 h. The brown solution was purified through membrane filtration and centrifugation. The final semi-solid form of CD exhibits a core size of 4.15–6.60 nm, with surface functionalities identified as –OH (3400 cm-1), -C=O (1750 cm-1), and epoxy (1050 cm-1), as confirmed by TEM and FTIR analyses. All these polar groups are substantial for homogeneous distribution of CDs in polymer matrix by enormous covalent and non-covalent bond formations. The WPU/CD nanocomposites were synthesized using an in-situ pre-polymerization process, wherein monomeric units and cyclodextrin were added sequentially to create the WPU/CD nanocomposite. The PU was synthesized in situ through a rearrangement reaction between polyisocyanate and polyol compounds, followed by the incorporation of CD into the polymer chain. Initially, Isophorone diisocyanate (IPDI), PEG600, and 2,2-bis(hydroxymethyl) propionic acid (BMPA) were reacted at a -NCO/-OH molar ratio of 1.5 at 80–85 °C for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere. Subsequently, polyol and butane diol were introduced into THF, maintaining a final NCO/OH ratio of 1, and the mixture was permitted to react at 60–65 °C for 3.5 h. Subsequently, CD was incorporated under identical reaction conditions and subjected to heating for 1.5 h. To neutralize the –COOH groups, triethylamine (TEA) was added at room temperature, followed by the introduction of water and the removal of tetrahydrofuran (THF) under reduced pressure. The resulting PU/CD nanocomposite was combined with epoxy resin (13 wt%) and fatty acid-based poly(amido amine) using mechanical stirring, followed by extended ultrasonication to ensure a homogeneous distribution. The solution was subsequently restored at 100 °C for 45 min under vacuum to produce the desired thermosetting nanocomposite [35].

Graphitic carbon nitride polymer (g-C3N4) has garnered significant attention in the last decade as a photocatalyst due to its metal-free composition, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, abundance, thermal and photochemical stability, favourable band gap (2.6 eV) for visible light absorption, and low potential for water splitting, O2/H2 generation, and CO2 photo-reduction. However, this polymer experiences poor visible spectrum photocatalytic performance due to its absorption below 430 nm, rapid electron-hole pair recombination, low quantum yield, poor electrical mobility, charge conductivity, and inefficient specific surface area. These factors further reduce its photocatalytic efficiency under the visible spectrum. In this context, defect site engineering, heterojunction formation, and nanocomposites are the alternative approaches. Here, CD/g-C3N4 polymer nanocomposite has shown impressive performance in terms of visible range photocatalysis, solar xenobiotic/dye/antibiotic degradation coupled with hydrogen gas production, etc. Here, the presence of carbon dots facilitates higher performance, which might be due to excellent electron transfer/reservoir properties, the presence of different emissive traps, excess orbitals that further impede the rapid recombination of charge carriers, the up-conversion luminescence property, etc. Several synthetic strategies for the synthesis of CD/g- C3N4 nanocomposites have been reported in the literature.

Recently, Mohamed et al. synthesised the graphitic carbon nitride/carbon quantum dot/bismuth oxychlorobromide nanocomposite (CN/CQD/BiOClBr) as a heterojunction photocatalyst by a calcination and hydrothermal method, then followed by mixing. This method enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency in the visible spectrum by altering the energy band structure and creating an up-conversion photocatalyst [36]. The CN/CQD/BiOClBr nanocomposite exhibits an 84.3 % degradation efficiency for tetracycline in wastewater and a 52 % mineralisation rate. The CQD was synthesised by a hydrothermal method. Melamine, serving as a precursor for carbon nitride (CN), was combined with an aqueous solution of CQD and subjected to heating at 550 °C for a duration of 3 h, with a heating rate of 3 °C/min. In summary, 0.2 g of CN/CQD powder was combined with ethylene glycol and 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3, along with halogen precursors KCl and KBr (totaling 1.0 mmol), and subjected to heating at 180 °C for 8 h to synthesize the CN/CQD/BiOClBr heterojunction (Fig. 3 ).

XRD analysis have shown similar diffraction peak for CN and CN/CQD which indicate the low CQD content in the composite. The diffraction peak at 27.08° is associated with the interplanar (002) spacing of 0.326 nm, whereas the weak signal at 13.17° for CN/CQD/BiOClBr indicates the successful formation of the nanocomposite. The incorporation of CQD enhanced the photocatalytic efficiency by diminishing the recombination of electron-hole pairs, as CQD serves as a sp2 state-like electron reservoir. The combination of BiOClBr and CQD deposition on the CN polymer surface enhances interfacial charge transfer efficiency and light harvesting capacity [36].

Nageib et al. developed a chitosan/silk fibroin nanofiber coated with a nitrogen-infused carbon quantum dot/α-tricalcium phosphate nanocomposite intended for application as a dressing material [37]. The di-ammonium hydrogen citrate aqueous solution was subjected to heating at 150 °C for 18 h within a hydrothermal bomb. The deposition of tri-calcium phosphate on N-CQD involved continuous stirring of 40 mg of TCP in 20 ml of as-synthesized N-CQD (10 mg/ml) for a duration of 2 h.

Next, a blend solution of CS/SF was prepared by mixing the stock solution of CS (4 % w/v) and SF (7 % w/v) under continuous stirring for 1 h. Then 5 % w/v N-CQG/α-TCP nanocomposite (Fig. 4 a, b) solution was added in stirring condition for 1 h, and further the homogeneous solution was electrospin with voltage of 21 Kv for a 0.7 mL/h feed rate, at 27 °C temperature (Fig. 4c). The mat was further processed by crosslinking in 3 % aqueous solution of glutaraldehyde in a desiccator for 12 h (Fig. 4d). The processed mat underwent cleaning with PBS and was subsequently stored at room temperature for one night. The structural and chemical characterization confirmed the formation of the N-CQD/TCP nanocomposite, resulting in modified nanofiber with an average diameter of 515±21 nm and a well-organized structure (Fig. 4). EDX and elemental mapping verified the uniform distribution of the nanocomposite on the fiber, which was attained via the electrospinning technique. The mechanical strength of the modified nanofiber has enhanced significantly due to the presence of OH-rich CQD, which reacts with chitosan and improves hydrogen bonding. Additionally, the modified nanofiber has demonstrated antibacterial wound healing properties in in vitro scratch analysis and in vivo studies using a rat model. It has been concluded that modified mats can provide hydrophilic, calcium, phosphate, and carbon-dot embedded surfaces along with chitosan, which is responsible for regeneration properties [37].

Wang et al. have designed CQD/Ag2S/CS nanocomposite as alternative antibiotics against pathogenic drug-resistant methicillin-resistant staphylococcus aurous strain (MRSA) [38]. The CQD component was prepared by a facile green synthesis approach using acetone as a precursor (Fig. 5 ). In the presence of NaOH, acetone undergoes base-catalysed aldol polymerisation followed by oligomerisation and the formation of a carbon-dot of 5–7 nm core and 10 nm height (Fig. 5) [33].

Next, CQD/Ag2S/CS was synthesized through in-situ scaling on a substrate of chitosan using PVP as a cross-linking. The TEM image revealed a spherical Ag2S nanoparticle with a lattice spacing of 0.268 nm, which corresponds to the (120) crystal plane of silver sulfide, surrounded by smaller particles with a measurement of 0.321 nm, aligning with the (002) crystal plane of CQDs. XPS and XRD analyses confirmed the formation of a nanocomposite. The bactericidal activity of nanocomposite involves destruction of the cell wall and cell membrane, followed by alteration of genetic material. Hence, the nanocomposite-mediated bactericidal activity does not show any drug resistance and has been anticipated as a beneficial approach compared to conventional antibiotics.

Ehtesabi et al. fabricated an alginate/chitosan/carbon dot (ACCDs) based nanocomposite sponge as wound dressing material [39]. CD was initially synthesized hydrothermally from whole pyracantha fruit in a stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for a duration of 6 h. The resultant CD is of 5–8 nm size with a predominant negative surface charge. A homogeneous gel solution for the nanocomposite sponge was prepared by blending a 2 % w/v aqueous sodium alginate solution with 1 % w/v chitosan in 1 % acetic acid. Then, 2 % glycerol was added as a plasticiser, carbon dioxide as a filler, and divalent Ca2+ as a cross-linker. The final nanocomposite ACCD sponge was prepared by lyophilization. The final composite sponge exhibits a brown colour when compared to the white AC sponge, indicating a uniform distribution of CD within the matrix. Morphological studies have shown that the incorporation of negatively charged carbon dots enhances the internal crosslinking by facilitating the positive/negative charge interaction and increasing the porosity of the sponge. This enhanced porosity in ACCD sponge compared to AC sponge increases the moisture content, provides a better scaffold for the attachment of blood cells and platelets, provides efficient oxygen and nutrient supply in the wound site, and accelerates the natural wound healing [39].

Zhou et al. developed a thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) for nanofiltration by integrating a carbon dot with hyperbranched zwitterionic polyethylenimine (CD-ZPEI0.6–10k) (Fig. 6 b) [40]. The core-shell CD-ZPEI were synthesized through hydrothermal treatment of citric acid with hyperbranched PEI of varying molecular weights at 200 °C for a duration of 10 h (Fig. 6a). The zwitterionic PEI (ZPEI) polymers were synthesized through the ring-opening reaction of 1,3-propanesultone, followed by a quaternization reaction with various PEI (Fig. 6a). The polymer-grafted CD displayed a broad peak centered at 19.7°, suggesting an amorphous core structure resulting from the low carbonization rate. The increase in average size from 1.5 to 2.2 nm suggests the presence of a thicker polymeric shell on the surface of CDs, transitioning from CD-ZPEI0.6 k to CD-ZPEI10 k.

The amide linkage between PEI and the CD surface was confirmed by the stretching vibration peaks of amino N−H at 3283 and 1564 cm−1, as well as the amide peak at 1640 cm−1. The XPS analysis verified the successful grafting of ZPEI onto the CD surface, preserving the chemical structure through a high-temperature hydrothermal process. The CD-ZPEI-TFN membrane was prepared by interfacial polymerisation reaction on the commercial microporous PSF substrate (Fig. 6b). Initially, a circular piece of PSF substrate was soaked in an aqueous solution of 1 wt% piparazine (PIP) and (0.1–0.9 %) wt% of CD-ZPEI solution for complete loading of the monomer and nanoparticle. The solution was subsequently transferred to the organic phase (hexane) containing 0.15 wt% TMC, resulting in the formation of a polyamide layer on the substrate. The modified membrane underwent heat treatment for 15 min at 60 °C to improve the cross-linking of the polyamide layer. The research conducted 1H nmR studies to confirm the quaternization of PEI to PS, resulting in the formation of zwitterionic polymers and their mobility with CDs. Fig. 6c illustrates the 1H nmR analysis of CDs integrated with ZPEI polymers. The peaks designated as a–e represent protons located at different positions within the polymer structure. Protons a and b are linked to carbon atoms at the α- and β-positions of the sulfonate groups, formed via the ring-opening reaction of PS molecules. Protons c–e are associated with carbon atoms at the α- and β-positions of the quaternary ammonium ions. After the grafting of ZPEI10k onto CDs, the characteristic peaks of ZPEI were still observable in the spectrum of CDs-ZPEI10k. Fig. 6d illustrates the FTIR spectrum of CDs with ZPEI. The adsorption peak at 3417 cm⁻¹, corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the O–H bond, confirms the formation of CDs via the pyrolysis of CA. Furthermore, peaks at 3280 and 1562 cm⁻¹, associated with the stretching vibrations of amino N–H, along with a peak at 1635 cm⁻¹ related to amide groups, demonstrated the presence of PEI polymers and their robust attachment to the CD surface through amidation reactions.

The incorporation of core-shell zwitterionic polymer-grafted carbon dots as nanofillers improved divalent salt rejection, water permeability, and antifouling properties of the TFN NF membrane. The impermeable inner core of CDs serves as an ultrafine carrier for the ZEPI shell, facilitating the formation of a compact and orderly 3D network of water channels for rapid water transport and salt ion rejection, whereas extensive zwitterionic outer polymeric shell enhances the antifouling and hydrophilicity characteristics of the TFN NF membrane (Fig. 7 ) [40].

Pal, S. has synthesized a polypyrrole grafted chitosan decorated CD nanocomposite for the enhanced catalytic efficiency under visible light illumination [41]. Initially, the grafted copolymer was prepared by free radical polymerization at a lower temperature when APS used as an initiator. Then the Ch-g-PPy has been added as a stabilising agent to the CD solution synthesised hydrothermally from water melon seed. In general, 2 mL of CDs solution was combined with the Ch-g-PPy solution under ice-cold conditions and stirred for 12 h to produce the Ch-g-PPy@CDs composite material. The nanocomposite was formed through significant interactions between the surface functional groups of CDs and the polymer, facilitated by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. The resulting nanocomposite demonstrates enhanced degradation of chlorophenols in aqueous solution when exposed to sunlight. The CD effectively absorbs the visible spectrum of sunlight owing to its lower band gap of 1.84 eV. The photoexcited electron is transferred to the lower orbital of PPy in the copolymer, thereby reducing the electron/hole recombination rate in CDs. Electrons in the LUMO of PPy can produce superoxide when exposed to molecular oxygen and hydroxyl radicals. High-energy species interact with toxic CP contaminants in water and undergo rapid degradation [41].

CDs represent a class of carbon-based nanoparticles characterized mainly by their amorphous and/or crystalline carbonaceous core regions. They typically display a quasi-spherical morphology, with particle sizes less than 10 nm and numerous functional groups (Fig. 8 a) [15,43–45]. Niu et al. reported the lattice spacing of the CDs to be between 0.178 nm and 0.238 nm, corresponding to various diffraction planes with an average uniform spherical shape of 8 nm (Fig. 8 b-e) [46]. Herein, the lattice space of the CDs in question is close to that of graphite, indicating the presence of the inherent crystalline structure of graphitic interlayer spacing. Meanwhile, Cao et al., reported the CDs material with about 5 nm particle size (Fig. 8 f) [47]. Based on their detailed structural characteristics, the carbon atoms in the core of CDs consist of predominant mixed sp2/Sp3 or Sp3 hybridized electronic structures containing oxygenated functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups (Fig. 8 a) which share many properties with other electron-rich carbon materials [48–52].

In addition to their small size, CDs typically exhibit outstanding water solubility, low toxicity, a large surface area, and facile surface functionalization. A comprehensive study by A. Bhattacharya et al. investigate the interaction between luminescent polymer nanodots (PNDs) and human serum albumin (HSA) in its free and ligand-bound states through spectrophotometric and calorimetric methods [51]. Fig. 9 A displays the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized PND. The prominent peak at 3422 cm⁻¹ is associated with the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH functional groups, whereas the peak at 1408 cm⁻¹ is linked to –CH₂ scissoring vibrations. The peaks at 1631 cm⁻¹ and 2930 cm⁻¹ correspond to the characteristic absorption bands of –N–H and –C–H stretching, respectively. Fig. 9B presents the HRTEM image of the synthesized PND, demonstrating a spherical morphology with a mean diameter of 4.68 ± 0.06 nm. The inset presents an enlarged depiction of an individual PND. The mean hydrodynamic diameter, determined through DLS measurements (Fig. 9C), is 6.24 ± 0.8 nm. Fig. 9D presents the powder XRD pattern of PND, featuring a broad peak at 2θ = 28.9° (d = 0.31 nm), which signifies its amorphous characteristics. Elemental analysis verifies the composition: 50.36 % carbon, 36.66 % oxygen, 7.76 % nitrogen, and 5.22 % hydrogen (Fig. 9D, inset). The ¹³C nmR spectrum (Fig. 9E) shows signals between 40–50 ppm, which are characteristic of aliphatic (sp³) carbon atoms, consistent with previously documented nmR data for PND. Significantly, the absence of peaks associated with aromatic carbon in the 125–150 ppm range confirms that the synthesized PND has a distinct structural composition compared to CDs. The photoluminescence quantum yield is established at 8.4 %, with quinine sulfate serving as the reference standard [53].

In this context, the inherent morphological, size, and structural properties of CDs and their functional groups provide incredible surface properties that can form interfaces and react with the polymer matrix. Ultimately, these incredible surface properties will lead to the improvement of various properties with unique morphologies, enabling the construction of polymer/CD nanocomposites for economical and practical applications. Commonly, the main polymer phase is used to passivate and stabilize the CDs, which will ultimately go through particle dissipation to present homogeneously dispersed CD particles within the polymer matrix and provide mechanical support [10,47]. Graphical illustration of this kind of polymer/CDs nanocomposite morphological structure is presented in Scheme 1 with some sort of hydrogen bonding mechanism. It is important to fully understand the morphology of the polymer/CD's nanocomposites, since it well-know that the morphological properties of polymers have a direct influence on their impact strength and other properties [54]. However, tailoring and controlling the morphological structure of the polymer without sacrificing the other inherent properties of filler particles or neat polymers is still not fundamentally understood. Therefore, a review paper is necessary to provide further insights into the morphological features, structural and chemical attributes, and advantages related to various properties of polymer/CD nanocomposites. Several efforts have been made to develop polymer/CD nanocomposites, such as polyvinyl alcohol (PVA)/CD nanocomposites films, for sensing applications, particularly tetracycline (TC) in real samples [55]. In this study, Hu et al. [55], reported the development of rougher PVA/CDs nanocomposite film with more porous structure attributes in relation to its neat PVA film (Fig. 10 I). However, the inherent nanofiber structural morphology of PVA is noted in both films. The incorporation of CD particles significantly impacts the porosity morphological structure, which is crucial for adsorption events, TC sensing in food analysis, and the enhancement of mechanical properties. Further insight reveals that the numerous functional groups on the surface of CDs significantly contribute to the strong interaction between the hydroxyl-rich PVA chain and the oxygen-containing functional groups of CDs. Most CD particles are situated on the left side of the PVA matrix, as illustrated in Scheme 1 and depicted in the SEM image in Fig. 10 II [56,57].

Furthermore, the many functional groups (-OH and -COOH) on the surfaces of the PVA matrix and CDs might undergo ionization at pH 12. This would enhance the negative charge density of PVA/CD nanocomposites, increasing the number of attraction sites and the adsorption effectiveness of positively charged molecules. El-Shamy and Zayied used the FE-SEM method to demonstrate that an increase in CD content may effectively regulate the surface roughness, morphological structure, and density of the PVA/CD nanocomposites. In conclusion, a significant concentration of CDs may provide a high packing density and an expanded surface area, attributable to enhance in pore volume and size inside the PVA matrix, resulting in a morphological transition from micro-porous to meso-porous (Fig. 11 A and D) [58]. Fig. 11 (E) presents the XRD patterns of green PVA, the synthesized CQDs, and PVA/CQDs nanocomposite films. A broad peak appears at 2θ = 23.7° for CQDs, indicating a highly disordered arrangement of carbon atoms associated with the C (002) plane, which corresponds to the hexagonal lattice structure of graphite. The calculated d-spacing for the C(002) plane of CQD nanoparticles is approximately 0.35 ± 0.01 nm. Moreover, the broadening of the XRD peak is attributed to the extremely small size of the synthesized CQD nanoparticles [58]. A resembling observation was claimed by Nenashev et al., where the morphology and structure of polythiophene-derivative poly(3,4ethylenedioxythiophene)/poly(4-styrenesulphonate) (PEDOT/PSS) filled with different concentrations of a highly optical and electrical CDs component [59]. The CD-based films exhibited a heterogeneous morphology, characterized by an inhomogeneous and rough surface. The carbon material exhibited a distinctive globular form, with globule diameters ranging from around 50 to 250 nm, contingent upon the concentration of the PEDOT:PSS mix in the composite material. Significantly, the (50 %) CDs/PEDOT:PSS nanocomposite, characterised by a higher concentration of CDs, exhibited a reduced average size within the range of 50–100 nm. Nenashev et al. discovered that elevating the concentration of CDs diminishes the particle heights inside the polymer matrix to 2–3 nm, 1–3 nm, and 0.5–1 nm for the CQDs/PEDOT:PSS at 3 %, 5 %, and 50 %, respectively, resulting in a significant alteration in porosity [59].

To date, the preparation of efficient light emitters has progressively drawn intense research and development attention in the lighting industry and photonics, polymer/CDs nanocomposites turn to be the ideal candidate due to the inherent optical properties of CDs particles [45,51]. In brief, CDs exhibits phenomenal characteristics such as high photostability, tunable excitation and emission, high solubility, good chemical inertness, and easy surface modification, which are needed for excellent optical and electrical performance [49,59,60]. CDs have significant two-photon activity, which has been shown to correspond closely with their pulsed laser stimulation in the near-infrared, resulting in intense luminosity in the visible spectrum. Cao et al. reported that the two-photon absorption cross-section of the CDs at 800 nm is around 39,000 GM, comparable to that of CdSe quantum dots, which ranges from 780 to 10,300 GM [47]. Importantly, CdSe quantum dots are known to be one of the best-performing semiconductors. Since, CDs are now described as a very valuable asset of nanotechnology, which addresses the weight, reactivity responses and effective functionalities researchers and optical industry might replace the CdSe component by CDs-based components. Furthermore, it is essential to acknowledge that the two-photon absorption cross-section values for CDs and CdSe fall within the range of 2000–47000 GM, as established for quantum dots [61]. Conversely, it is established that CDs can be passivated by various ligands, especially amines, which may improve their luminescent properties. For example, Zhang et al., stated the CDs with different N contents prepared by using CCl4 and NaNH2 as base materials, exhibited tunable luminescence with high quantum yields [62]. In this rational, N doping CDs synthesis strategy enhance luminescent properties more significant than that of controlling the CDs particle size to become very small, therefore, N doping is crucial which effect the optical properties. In addition, the N doped CDs-based material could be stored for longer period (at least 6 months) with minimal reduction in the intensity of photoluminescence emission. Consequently, the primary roles of such dopants are to influence the electrical structure, bandgap, and optical characteristics of carbon dots by altering the energy levels (π-π*) [63]. Current knowledge regarding carbon dots (CDs) with organic moieties on their surface morphology indicates that the passivated CDs exhibit strong photoluminescence in both solution and solid states, with emissions occurring in the visible wavelength range and extending into the near-infrared (Fig. 12 (I) (a, b)). Fig. 12 (I) (c) illustrates the extensive photoluminescence spectra of the carbon dots, which are contingent upon excitation wavelengths. The observed behavior is attributed to the influence of different particle sizes within the sample and the distribution of unique emissive sites on each passivated carbon dot, leading to the vivid and multicolored light emissions noted [10]. Conversely, several investigations have shown that quantum-confinement effects result in longer emission wavelengths for larger-sized carbon dots (CDs). Bouknaitir et al. previously elucidated the optical properties of PMMA/CDs nanocomposites, revealing that absorbance is directly proportional to the C-Dots fraction, indicating that the nanoparticles retain their distinctiveness within the polymer matrix and do not experience significant aggregation [44]. The photoluminescence spectra of the composites display a broad emission band that shifts to lower energy with increasing excitation wavelength, attributed to the embedded carbon dots.

Simultaneously, fluorescence may also occur; in this instance, the fluorescence in carbon dots (CDs) might arise from ether fluorescence emissions due to bandgap transitions of conjugated π-domains or surface defects [65]. Deng et al. [66] documented the functionality of C=O bonds on the surface of carbon dots (CDs) and hydroxyl groups inside the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix, resulting in a phosphorescent emission peak at 500 nm with an average lifespan of 380 ms under 325 nm stimulation. This peak was attributed to limited intramolecular motions and the prevention of nonradiative relaxation. The above-mentioned work shows that the morphology and structural properties play a critical role in determining the optical properties; hence, a self-explanatory schematical diagram based on the phosphorescence process of carbon dots dispersed in the PVA matrix is presented to gain more insights based on the reported observations (Scheme 1). Nevertheless, phosphorescence could also be promoted in this case, because PVA exhibits a good oxygen barrier property, this will eventually hinder the direct collision between aromatic carbonyls and oxygen functional groups [66]. Jiang et al. [57] previously shown that high-performance polymer/CDs light-emitting flexible films with submicron-structured patterns may attain an internal quantum yield of around 61 % and enhance external light-extraction efficiency while exhibiting commendable photostability. More recently, a quantum yield of about 47 % was reported for the PVA/CD-based film. The PVA/CDs films exhibit excitation-independent emission and have a Cd2+ removal effectiveness of 91.1 % [64]. Zheng et al., [67], reported the highest quantum yield of 93.3 % resulting from the preparation of CDs with citric and tris-(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris) and the fluorescence lifetime of the CDs is up to 19.50 ns. This unlocks the efficiency of the CDs brightness as shown in Fig. 12 (II) (a-d) and serve as an indication of great potential applications of CDs particles in various optical materials and devices [64]. The optical properties of CDs are fundamentally understood to vary and depend on their surface chemistry, which includes solubility effects. Therefore, more active reaction sites improve structural stability and enhance electronic conductivity, which favours charge transfer properties.

To develop hybrid materials that are often rapidly cleared from circulation, it is crucial to address challenges related to short blood half-lives and limited tumor penetration near blood vessels. Sung et al. introduced a sponge-inspired carbon composite that utilizes a red blood cell (RBC) membrane, functioning as a stealth agent and a photolytic carrier for tumor-penetrative agents, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and docetaxel (DTX) [68]. GQDs exhibited strong blue photoluminescence, emitting around 450 nm. Atomic force microscopy revealed a thickness of approximately 1 nm, potentially due to low surface defects. Additionally, GQDs effectively absorbed DTX via π–π interactions.

Wang et al. synthesized silica-loaded carbon dots conjugated with folic acid (SiO₂@CDs-FA) through a simple one-pot method, building on the research conducted by Sung et al. The nanoparticles demonstrated stable blue fluorescence and were readily separable from the solution, utilizing SiO₂ spheres as carriers, as illustrated in Fig. 13 (a, b). The complex was specifically internalized by folate-positive HeLa cells through receptor-mediated endocytosis, while 293T cells lacking folate receptors did not uptake it. The research highlighted the potential of nanoparticles for large-scale production and their biological properties, which are critical for clinical applications [57]. Nasrin et al. developed novel ternary-doped carbon dots via a hydrothermal process, conjugated with FA. The nanoparticles exhibited a wide multi-color emission spectrum, excellent photostability, biocompatibility, and effective cellular uptake in folate receptor-positive SKMEL28 melanoma cells. Their findings underscore the potential applications of these nanoparticles in imaging, diagnosis, and phototherapy for melanoma [69]. Jiang et al. [57] demonstrated that high-performance polymer/CDs light-emitting flexible films featuring submicron-structured patterns can achieve an internal quantum yield of approximately 61 %. These films also improve external light-extraction efficiency and display notable photostability, as illustrated in Fig. 13 (c, d).

The distribution and the degree of particle dispersion are crucial together with the percolation threshold for super electric conductivity performance. In fact, the major purpose of the homogenously dispersed particles is to improve the conductivity, optical, and other characteristics of the polymer nanocomposite material. The primary reason for utilizing particles is their high surface-to-volume ratio, which enhances interaction with the polymer matrix, thus improving the overall effectiveness of the polymer nanocomposite material [71,72]. In addition, due to charge carriers hopping between localized states, the response of the polymer nanocomposites material can exhibit aberrant low-frequency dispersion for loading beyond the percolation threshold.

Insight information regarding conduction mechanisms, polarization, and charge mobility of the polymer/CDs nanocomposites is vital when examining the electrical properties of a composite material. Ansary et al. [72] prepared the PMMA/CD nanocomposites loaded with 2.5 wt.% and 4 wt.% CDs particles for improved electrical properties below the percolation threshold. The study identified two conducting mechanisms that adhere to a double power law regarding the relationship between electrical conductivity and frequency. The dipolar interactions in these nanocomposites indicated through frequency-dependent electric modulus the existence of dual dielectric relaxation processes: the initial process, occurring at low frequencies, was ascribed to conduction effects, while the subsequent process, manifesting at high frequencies, was linked to interfacial polarization. A modification in the activation energy (Ea) at room temperature revealed that the Arrhenius law regulates the temperature dependence of AC conductivity. For example, Nenashev et al. [59] prepared a series of PEOT: PSS/CDs-based nanocomposites a demonstrated that PEOT: PSS/CDs (50 %) nanocomposites obtained Ea value of about 7.5 meV. Fig. 14 represents the current vs voltage graph and resistivity parameters for different compositions of PEOT: PSS/CDs.

The distinctive Ea outcome is associated with the elevated concentration of PEDOT:PSS, which the authors contend resulted in the irregular distribution of agglomerates and the available surface area of CDs inside the polymer matrix, facilitating the primary transit of charge carriers in these systems. This relates to the hopping mechanism of charge carriers contributing to conductivity among impurity states within the bandgap. This behavior is feasible due to the use of conductive polymers, namely the PEDOT:PSS system, as a conductive matrix and for the injection of p-type layers in polymers and composites. The molecular weight of the conductive conjugate polymer PEDOT, which ranges from 1000 to 2500, and the PSS chain, which is approximately 400,000, play a significant role. This demonstrates the primary importance of the increased molecular weight of the polymer matrix; generally, increasing molecular weight and the content of PEDOT with the PSS system result in a combined effect of enhanced conductivity at large [59,73]. Analysis indicates that the PEDOT:PSS/CDs (50 %) nanocomposite demonstrates enhanced stability, suggesting its applicability as a reliable hole transport layer in flexible organic electronic devices. The unique properties of PEDOT: PSS/CD nanocomposites, combined with their molecular weight and surface morphology structure change, have contributed to their high level of importance, strengthening the hole transport mechanism. Nonetheless, significant polymers like polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) might be used alongside CDs owing to their intrinsic multi-semicrystalline structures, which encompass polar (β-, γ-, δ-, and ε-) and non-polar (α-) phases for electrical and magnetic applications. Badaw et al. [74] observed that as the wt% ratio of PVDF/CDs increased from 0 to 10 %, the direct energy band gap of the PVDF/CDs-based nanocomposite films decreased from 5.28 to 2.96 eV, which can be attributed to the formation of additional low-energy states.This behaviour validates the effective integration of CDs and the robust connection between the CD particles and the multi-semicrystalline PVDF polymer matrix, resulting in the high-performance PVD/CD nanocomposite material.

CDs-filled materials are finding an increasing number of applications as catalyst components. Factors such as their excellent conductivity, diverse functional groups, high level of crystallinity, and ease of production make them an attractive choice. In summary, the surface morphology structure of CDs, with its presence of negative charges and diverse functional groups such as OH, COOH, and NH2, provides strong coordination sites for bonding with transition metal ions, resulting in improved catalytic effects. This indicates that integrating carbon dots with porous materials or doping carbon dots with heteroatoms has significant practical potential for carbon dot-based components. In conclusion, CDs are presently integrated with porous materials including zeolites, carbon-based porous substances, porous graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄), mesoporous SiO₂, porous metallic compounds, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), demonstrating outstanding performance and diverse applications. Porous materials serve to scatter and stabilize carbon dots (CDs); however, their inherent physical and chemical properties, along with the unique characteristics of CDs, may enhance performance or enable innovative applications [75]. CD particles can be doped with various heteroatoms, such as boron, nitrogen, fluorine, phosphorus, and sulfur, which may enhance catalytic activity by promoting electron transport through internal interactions, thus improving optical and electronic properties [48,76]. For example, Zuo et al. [77] the hydrothermal process produced F-doped carbon dots, showcasing increased quantum yields and enhanced electron transport for the best photocatalytic application. In a comprehensive investigation, Li et al. [78] used CD particles doped with hydrogen sulfate groups (S-CDs) for visible light responsiveness and photocatalytic activities. S-CDs enabled the ring-opening of epoxides in the presence of nucleophiles, methanol, and other primary alcohol solvents. The process shows that visible light irradiation induces the ionization of the SO3H group, releasing more protons and generating a more acidic environment that improves the reaction, resulting in increased product yield and selectivity relative to reactions conducted without light. Furthermore, photoexcitation and charge separation in CDs elicit an electron-withdrawing action from the acidic groups.

Furthermore, the excellent conductivity of the base catalysts typically results in higher energy conversion efficiency in the electrocatalytic reaction. Hence, the better conductivity of CDs can promote electron transfer in electrochemical reactions, increasing the reaction rate [77]. The P-CDs that are doped have a low level of carbonization, which results in a low level of crystallinity. Nonetheless, numerous organic molecules and polymer chains persist on the surface of the carbon core, resulting in inadequate conductivity and obstructing the advancement of electrocatalytic reactions. In contrast, GQDs and CDs with increased carbonization display distinct characteristics, including graphite lattice fringes, and demonstrate superior conductivity akin to graphene [79]. To comprehend the catalytic properties of carbon-based nanomaterials, it is essential to compute the free energy changes for the fundamental oxygen reduction steps on the optimal catalyst. Wang et al. [80] computed the free energy variations of graphene, CDs, P-graphene, and P-CDs catalysts throughout the basic oxygen reduction phase. They intentionally assumed that the adsorbed species (*) are consistently attached to the phosphorus site in the P-doped material, while other intermediates, such as OH, are connected to the neighboring carbon atoms, as seen in Fig. 15 (free energy diagram). The oxygen reduction in these catalysts is a thermodynamically favourable process owing to the decrease in free energy. The production of *OOH on planar graphene has a minor uphill free energy, suggesting that the oxygen reduction process is feasible, serves as a rate-limiting step, and requires an external potential for activation. Conversely, the other three catalysts do not exhibit the same increase in energy, since the free energy constantly diminishes at each stage. Conversely, P-doped CDs are the most favoured catalysts for the oxygen reduction process, attributed to their substantial downhill free energy for each reaction step, corroborated by O2 dissociation study [80]. Doped carbon dots have consistently shown favourable results in photocatalytic applications, but mono-component carbon dots display inadequate photocatalytic activity owing to their low photosensitization efficiency [75].

Polymer-based CD nanocomposites contain layer double hydroxide (LDH) and were successfully prepared by Yang et al. [81] for highly efficient heterogeneous catalytic reduction as shown in a typical Schematic 2 diagram. CDs functioned as a reductant and stabilizer after the in situ deposition of metal Pd onto LDH@PDA@PNIPAM. The LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd/CDs nanohybrid catalyst showed superior efficacy in the catalytic reduction of several dyes and nitrophenols, compared to the LDH@PDA@PNIPAM@Pd catalyst without CD modification. The observed impact stemmed from the synergistic catalytic activity of the metal active center combined with CDs and LDH, together with their enhanced dispersion stability. Methylene blue demonstrates significant catalytic reduction activity, shown by a high turnover frequency of 545.2 min⁻¹. Moreover, the presence of PNIPAM polymer chains endowed the nanohybrid with remarkable thermoresponsive catalytic capabilities. On the other hand, Gu et al. [82] synthesized a metal-free bifunctional catalyst, PANI/CDs, using nickel foam (NF). PANI serves as a conductive carrier to address the issue of insufficient proton availability in the neutral electrolyte. The CDs enhance the catalyst's activity in the presence of visible light. This configuration lowers the overpotentials for the oxygen evolution and hydrogen evolution processes on PANI/CDs/NF in the neutral electrolyte by 150 mV and 65 mV, respectively, yielding current densities of 30 mA cm⁻² and 20 mA cm⁻². The present density of PANI/CDs/NF in a fully operational water-splitting cell attains 13.27 mA cm⁻² at 2.0 V under visible light, indicating a 62.8 % enhancement relative to the dark condition (8.15 mA cm⁻²). Notably, typical composites may effectively solve the limitations of pure CDs by improving a variety of qualities such as catalytic, electrical, and optical. The tiny size and surface area of CDs' numerous functional groups allow for robust contact with polymers and effective CD property transmission. Polymer/CD nanocomposites exhibit potential applications across various fields, including environmental science, biomedical engineering, and energy storage.

CDs have demonstrated an exciting prospect in a variety of applications attributed to the simple preparation, low toxicity, good optical performance, high chemical stability, eco-friendliness, biocompatibility, bright fluorescence, low-cost precursors, and diversity in compositions and structures [83]. All of these advantageous properties, together with the plethora of surface polar functions, have given CDs enormous potential for future development into polymer/CD nanocomposite materials, which may result in major performance increases and a wide range of novel applications. Polymer/CD nanocomposite materials have lately gained popularity in a number of sectors, including energy storage, environmental protection, supercapacitors, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), and lithium-sulphur batteries (LSBs) [84,85].

The utilisation of CDs for pollutant sensing and treatment has showed considerable promise because of unique properties [86]. The application of polymer/CD nanocomposites in environmental contexts will be categorized into two sections: the first addressing metal ion sensing and the second examining fluorescent probing. The superior advantages of composite materials, including enhanced stability, portability, recyclability, and functionality, have led to a rise in research focusing on polymer/CD nanocomposites as opposed to CDs in isolation.

The detection of metal ions, particularly heavy metal ions, is crucial for safeguarding human health and the environment. One of the most dangerous environmental pollutants, heavy metals, seriously harms both animal and human health. One of the best materials for identifying heavy metal ions is CDs [87]. For example, CDs coated carboxymethyl cellulose/ hydroxyapatite nanocomposite was made by Sarkar et al. [88] via the one-pot synthesis method for Fe(III) detection. The obtained nanocomposite exhibits excellent sensing abilities of Fe(III) with a linear range (0.2–1.0 μM) and a LOD (55 nm). The resulting nanocomposite demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe(III) ions, as well as good osteogenic characteristics for bone tissue engineering. The resultant nanocomposite exhibited high stability with an intense blue emission at 440 nm. The resulting nanocomposite could be promising in various biomedical fields, including ion sensing, medication delivery, and bone tissue engineering.

Saikia and Karak [89] reported on the creation of a polyaniline nanofiber/CD nanocomposite using a hydrothermal method and in situ polymerization, which served as an excellent fluorimetric sensor for As3+. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibited significant antioxidant properties, effectively scavenging up to 86 % of 2,2-diphenyl picryl hydrazyl (DPPH) radicals within a timeframe of less than 30 min. The resultant nanocomposite may be useful for fluorimetric As3+ detection in polluted water as well as a potent antioxidant. Whereas, according to chitosan (CS), CQDs, and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), nanocomposite was synthesized by Song et al. [90] via hydrothermal method for detecting I-ions. The produced nanocomposite displays high I-selectivity, and a linear relationship between DA and I-concentrations in the range of 20–140 μM was discovered. 140–400 μM and a LOD (2.3 μM). Therefore, the obtained nanocomposite could be a promising candidate for I-ion detection. Another study by Saleviter et al. [91] developed a CS-graphene oxide/cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs) nanocomposite for the detection of cobalt ions (Co2+) using SPR spectroscopy. The produced nanocomposite possesses outstanding Co2+ sensitivity and sensing capability. The linear range is established between 0.01 and 10 ppm, with a detection limit of 0.1188 ppm-1. The SPR results aligned most closely with the Sips model, indicating a binding affinity of 0.939. The generated nanocomposites may be a suitable choice for detecting Co2+ using SPR spectroscopy. Zhao et al. [92] developed a highly luminous nitrogen-coated carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)/CS nanocomposite through a one-step hydrothermal process for the detection of Fe³⁺. The resultant nanocomposite has fluorescence that is wavelength dependent, with maximum excitation and emission at 330 and 410 nm, respectively. Such reliable and economical carbon materials have the potential to be used for Fe³⁺ monitoring in environmental applications, as shown by the nanocomposite's outstanding sensitivity and selectivity.

According to Ahmadian-Fard-Fini et al. [93], cellulose acetate nanofibers/Fe/CDs nanocomposite was prepared using a hydrothermal technique for Hg2+ and pb2+ detection. Due to the magnetic filler's ability to easily gather harmful heavy metals, which can be found in areas like industrial waste water, the obtained nanocomposite for their detection is not dangerous and does not pose any risk due to its nanoscale. Echabaane et al. [94] conducted a study in which they prepared a CDs/CS nanocomposite as an impedimetric sensor for the detection of Cu2+. CDs were created by simply heating a glucose-aqueous solution that was acidic. With a high sensitivity and a very low limit of detection of 5 × 10−10 M, the resulting nanocomposite-based sensor has demonstrated good stability, selectivity, and reproducibility. The resulting sensor, a highly sensitive Cu2+ sensor, offers a potentially economically advantageous nanoplatform for applications involving water monitoring. Boronic acid-modified magnetic CS quantum dot nanocomposite was created by Rafiee et al. [95] via hydrothermal treatment for Cu2+ detection‏. The obtained nanocomposite has excellent sensitivity and sensing abilities of Cu2+ with linear range (1.0–30.0 μM) and a LOD (0.3 μM) through the boronic-acid moiety's interaction with Cu2+. In comparison to several metal cations (M2+, M = mg, Ni, Zn, and Pb), the produced nanocomposite demonstrated superior sensitivity to copper ions.

Carbon quantum dots/polyaniline (CQDs/PANI) nanocomposites were created by Singh and Singh [96] via the one-step complexation reaction process for identifying Hg (II). The final nanocomposites range in LOD and LOQ for Hg2+ from 0.017 to 0.031 ppm, respectively. The obtained nanocomposites exhibit good thermal stability (up to 750 °C), selectivity, and reproducibility, with a high sensitivity for Hg(II) detection. The obtained nanocomposites could be promising candidates for Hg(II) detection in environmental applications.

Godoi et al. [97] conducted a study in which they developed an agarose hydrogel incorporated with carbon dots (CD). This sensing system enables the colorimetric-optical detection of five heavy metal ions: Cr6+, Cu+, Fe3+, Pb+, and Mn+. The observed color changes in the Agr/CD hydrogel thin film aligned with the hue of the corresponding metal ion solution. The observed color shift may result from the deliberate arrangement of colored chitosan metal chelates. According to their assertions, the fabricated Agr/CD hydrogel has a practical advantage in terms of its ease of handling and on-site use when compared to other sensing platforms. In a separate study, Kumar et al. [98] performed a one-pot synthesis in which carbon dots (CDs) were put onto Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) that were encapsulated by carboxymethyl chitosan. The fluorescence properties of magnetic nanoparticles exhibit remarkable specificity in the detection of Cu2+ ions relative to the recognition of other metal ion species. The introduction of Cu2+ ions at different concentrations has been shown to result in a decrease in fluorescence intensity.

In another study, Wang et al. successfully synthesised yellow-emitting carbon dots (y-CDs) using a highly efficient one-step hydrothermal method [99]. They were able to develop a composite sensor using a combination of y-CDs and polymers. This innovative sensor has been fabricated in two distinct forms. The y-CDs have been expertly infused with a unique blend of either carboxymethylcellulose and polyvinyl alcohol or chitosan. Their results revealed that both configurations demonstrated selectivity. However, an intriguing finding emerged: the sensor response of y-CDs infused with chitosan films exhibited significantly higher sensitivity when compared to y-CDs infused with carboxymethylcellulose and polyvinyl alcohol films.
