This discovery highlights the potential of chitosan films to enhance the sensitivity of y-CDs-based sensors. The observed phenomenon was attributed to the chelation mechanism between chitosan and Cu2+ ions [99]. A recent study conducted by De Silva et al. included the development of a unique fluorescent film. This film was developed by blending carboxymethyl cellulose and sodium alginate polymers, which were then combined with carbon dots [100]. The film exhibited exceptional optical and mechanical characteristics. Moreover, the films exhibited improved detection capabilities for Ag⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions in both potable and lacustrine water samples. The detection range for silver and iron (III) ions was determined to be 50–500 μM, with detection limits of 29 μM and 24 μM, respectively. The film demonstrated sufficient effectiveness in detecting Ag⁺ and Fe³⁺ ions in both synthetic solutions and actual samples. In a separate work, Rahmani and Ghaemy [101] synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) that are water-soluble through a hydrothermal treatment technique using tragacanth gum and ethylenediamine. The test exhibited notable sensitivity and selectivity for Au³⁺ ions, achieving a low detection limit of 2.69 μM and a linear dynamic range from 0 to 100 μM. Zhang et al. [102] developed a composite material consisting of ZIF-8 and xylan-derived carbon dots. This composite was then used to create an immobilized carbon fiber membrane for selectively detecting Cu2+ ions in real-world samples using fluorescence techniques. The carbon fiber membrane exhibited significant blue fluorescence, indicating a high level of sensitivity and selectivity. This material served as a fluorescence sensor platform for Cu2+ detection, achieving a detection limit of 3.48 nm. Moreover, it has been shown that the material exhibits a notable level of biocompatibility, making it suitable for applications involving the detection of Cu2+ concentrations in real-world samples. The recovery rate achieved in such applications falls within the range of 97 % to 112 %. Recently, Luo et al. [103], synthesised and grafted green fluorescence CDs onto the surface of red-emission semiconducting polymer nanoparticles via electrostatic interaction. A notable positive correlation was observed across the concentration range of 0–100 μM, with the minimum concentration for reliable detection of copper ions estimated at 0.577 μM using CDs-based nanohybrids as a radiometric probe. The results shown in Fig. 16 A indicate that the test strips, which were loaded with nanohybrid probes based on CDs, exhibited a coral color when exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm. As the concentrations of the copper solution applied to the strip advance from 0 to 200 μM, a progressive darkening of the color of the test strip is seen, transitioning from a coral hue to a dark pink shade. The RGB values of the test strips were recorded using a smartphone application known as a colour recognizer. Subsequently, the intensity ratio between the green-to-red channel ratio (G/R) was computed. Fig. 16B illustrates a significant linear correlation (R2 = 0.936) between the G/R ratio and Cu2+ concentration within the range of 5–80 μM. The probe exhibited multiple advantages, including ease of manufacturing, excellent water solubility, increased sensitivity, and a broad linear response range. The CDs-based nanohybrid probe was successfully employed for the detection of Cu2+ in actual water samples.

The use of CDs as fluorescent probes to quantitatively detect the presence of pollutants has received widespread evaluation. Due to their clear advantages, including strong fluorescence characteristics, absorption area, excellent photostability, and water solubility, CDs have sparked a tremendous deal of interest and have been widely used in the chemical sensing field. Furthermore, CDs have advantages such as low toxicity, superior biocompatibility, affordability, straightforward preparation, and environmental sustainability [102]. CDs are thus a beneficial component for the advancement of fluorescence sensor systems. The CDs are frequently distributed in an aqueous solution to create liquid-state fluorescent probes; however, fluorescent probes can also be developed exclusively from CDs [104]. Nevertheless, there are clear disadvantages to this, such as limited portability and a low recovery efficiency. Moreover, the dispersion stability of CDs may be harmed by the interaction of other unclear factors, such as the pH and salt concentration in real aquatic environments, which could cause them to aggregate [105]. This could lead to the occurrence of the fluorescence self-quenching phenomenon. To solve these issues, CDs have been included in the polymer matrix to create solid-state fluorescent probes. Furthermore, engaging with the active sites of the polymer through secondary interactions or covalent bonding allows for the uniform distribution of CDs within a chosen polymer matrix, thereby effectively mitigating the agglomeration issue and enhancing the stability and accuracy of the analysis [15]. Cheng et al. [106] described the synthesis of a smart fluorescent polyethyleneimine (PEI)/microcrystalline cellulose (MCC)/carbon dots (CDs) nanocomposite (Fig. 17 a) using a one-step hydrothermal method for the detection of Fe3+‏. The TEM analysis of PEI/CDs demonstrates a spherical morphology, exhibiting an average diameter of 7.8 nm (Fig. 17b). Under 360 nm excitation, the photoluminescence emission of the produced nanocomposite transitioned from bright blue to faint blue in a similar manner (Fig. 17c). The synthesized nanocomposite exhibited enhanced thermal mechanical properties and demonstrated a stable, intense blue emission at 445 nm. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibited high accuracy and stability within a linear range of 10–100 mM, with a detection limit of 65 nm. The synthesized nanocomposite demonstrates potential as a fluorescent probe for detecting Fe³⁺ in aqueous environments.
Shao et al. [107] detailed the synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs)/poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) nanocomposite through freezing/thawing techniques for the detection of Fe3+‏.

The obtained nanocomposite exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity, with a suitable linear range (10–100 mM) and a LOD (10 μm). Due to the fluorescence properties of CNDs, the produced nanocomposite displays fluorescence quenching in the presence of Fe3+. CDs/sodium alginate nanocomposite was synthesised by Ehtesabi et al. [108] for tetracycline (TC) detection. CDs were manufactured via a simple and inexpensive method. By applying pyrolysis, CDs with blue luminous emission were created. The obtained nanocomposite reduces the CD fluorescence intensity when combined with small amounts of TC. The obtained nanocomposite could be a promising candidate as a fluorescent probe for tetracycline detection in environmental TC pollutants.

Fluorescent lignin/ cellulose nanofibers/CDs nanocomposite was prepared by Yuan et al. [109] via radical polymerization for highly efficient detection and adsorption of Cr6+. The obtained nanocomposite exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, and sensitivity with a suitable linear range (15–200 mg/L) and a LOD (11.2 mg/L). The synthesized nanocomposite exhibits porous structures and shows sorption performance consistent with Freundlich and pseudo-second-order models, attaining a maximum adsorption capacity of 599.9 mg/g. Li et al. [110] detailed the synthesis of an amino-modified cellulose membrane that integrates a pyrene boronic acid and CD nanocomposite via hydrothermal carbonization, with the objective of detecting and removing fluoride F-ions at relevant ambient concentrations. The produced nanocomposite demonstrated great sensitivity, high selectivity, and sensitivity for F concentrations with an appropriate linear range (0–200 M) (R2 = 0.996), a LOD of 5.9 × 10−5 M, and a removal efficiency of 90.2 %. The obtained nanocomposite exhibited an MTT assay for the membrane, demonstrating excellent cytocompatibility. By simply replacing the existing probe with suitable replacements for different purposes, the produced nanocomposite could be a promising candidate for the development of new sensors.

Yellow-Emitting CDs Impregnated carboxymethylcellulose/PVA and CS nanocomposite was prepared by Wu et al. [99]. Wu et al. used a one-step hydrothermal method to detect Cus ions. There is a chelation mechanism between chitosan and Cu2+ ions. The obtained nanocomposite exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, and sensitivity for Cu2+ with a LOD of 10 nm/1.3 ppm.

Zhang et al. [111] synthesized a new PNIPAAm-CD nanocomposite for continuous fluorescence detection and Fe3+ adsorption by the copolymerization of PCDs with NIPAAm monomers. The synthesized nanocomposites have photoluminescent properties, enhanced adsorption efficacy for Fe3+, and a significant quenching response to metallic iron ions. The synthesized nanocomposite may serve as a good option for both adsorption and optical detection of Fe3+ ions. The CDs/CS nanocomposite was synthesized by Mohammadi et al. [112] using a Schiff base reaction to detect microRNA-21 in MCF-7 cancer cells. Yang et al. [113] described the synthesis of innovative fluorescent carbon dots/carboxylated nanocellulose (CNC) via the EDC/NHS coupling technique for the identification and elimination of Pb2+ and Fe3+ ions. The synthesized nanocomposites exhibit remarkable stability and sensitivity for Fe3+, attaining a LOD of 62.5 mg/L. Additionally, both Fe3+ and Pb2+ demonstrate Qmax of 98.3 and 442.0 mg/g, respectively.

A nitrogen-doped polymer/CDs nanocomposite was synthesized by Qiu et al. [114] using a one-step hydrothermal technique for the detection of Co2+ (Fig. 18 a). The mechanism of obtained nanocomposite as shown in Fig. 18b. The obtained nanocomposite improved of fluorescence quench. According to TEM of obtained nanocomposite, showed spherical NPs with PS of 9.88 ± 0.42 nm and excellent monodispersity (Fig. 18c). The obtained nanocomposite exhibits the excellent sensing abilities of Co2+ with linear range 0–200 μM and a LOD (1.0023 μM). The obtained nanocomposites exhibit extraordinary selectivity with small sample size. The obtained nanocomposite could be promising candidate for Co2+ detection in water.

In a recent study, Luo et al. [115] developed a chitosan-based hydrogel incorporating titanate, carbon dots, and cellulose nanofibers in a recent study. The objective of their research was to enhance the detection and removal efficiency of Cr(VI). The testing results indicated that the chitosan-based hydrogel with fluorescent properties exhibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 228.2 mg/g. The quantitative detection of Cr(VI) was established within a linear range of 10–80 mg/L. The integration of carbon dots within the porous structure enhanced the sensitivity of fluorescence detection, as reported by the scientists. Guo et al. [116] Guo et al. [114] synthesized a novel fluorescent nanocellulosic hydrogel incorporating carbon dots. This advanced hydrogel demonstrates significant effectiveness as an adsorbent for heavy metal removal. The synthesized hydrogel exhibited maximum adsorption capacities of 769 mg/g for Fe3+, 212 mg/g for Ba2+, 2056 mg/g for Pb2+, and 1246 mg/g for Cu2+. The nanocellulosic hydrogel exhibited a bright blue fluorescence, characterized by a fluorescence quantum yield of 23.6 %. Furthermore, it exhibited notable sensitivity in the detection of Fe³⁺ ions. Zhang et al. [117] synthesized a hydrogel composed of cellulose nanofibrils, starch, and carbon dot nanoparticles through a free-radical polymerization method, facilitating the simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). The novel luminous starch-based hydrogel exhibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 265.9 mg/g, representing a 13.0-fold increase compared to pure starch. The adsorption process conformed to the Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models, as demonstrated by the isotherm and kinetics analyses. The starch-based hydrogel functioned as a fluorescence sensor for Pb(II) measurement, attaining a detection limit of 0.06 g/L. The research indicates that Pb(II) ions were first adsorbed onto the surface of the novel fluorescent starch-based hydrogel via electrostatic attraction. The three-dimensional porous structure of the hydrogel enabled the migration of Pb(II) ions from the surface to the interior by creating channels for ion transport. During this movement process, numerous Pb(II) ions attached to the unique three-dimensional porous structure of the fluorescent starch-based hydrogel through complexation, ion exchange, and electrostatic interactions. The carbon dots within the hydrogel's three-dimensional porous structure functioned as a fluorescent source for detecting Pb(II) ions. In a separate work, Mahmoud et al. [118] functionalized carbon quantum dots onto a polymeric nanocomposite to enhance the microwave adsorptive absorption of vanadium (V) ions from an aqueous medium. Adsorption tests were conducted to evaluate the nano adsorbent's efficacy in removing vanadium (V) ions from an aqueous solution via microwave irradiation. Their synthesized nano adsorbent exhibited exceptional adsorption and selectivity for vanadium (V) ions from an acidic environment at pH 2.0, as shown by their sorption studies. The pseudo-second order adsorption kinetics of V(V) were found to be satisfactory, achieving a maximum capacity of 219 mg/g. A novel beads nanocomposite was developed in a separate study through the crosslinking of zinc oxide with alginate, graphene quantum dots, and carbon foam [119]. The prepared bead nanocomposites were utilized as absorbents for the removal of methylene blue (MB) dye and lead ions (Pb(II)) from aqueous solutions through batch experiments. The The results demonstrated that the optimal pH for MB was 7, whereas for Pb(II) it was 6. The beads nanocomposite exhibited a maximum adsorption capacity of 91.09 mg/g for methylene blue (MB) and 133.8 mg/g for lead ions (Pb(II)) in the conducted experiment. The studied beads nanocomposite exhibited effective reusability across five consecutive regeneration cycles. The developed nanocomposite beads demonstrated significant efficacy in removing Pb(II) and MB contaminants from real water samples, achieving removal rates of 100 % and 92.2 %, respectively. The synthesis of carbon quantum dots on poly(anthranilic acid-formaldehyde-phthalic acid) (CQDs@PAFP) has been achieved using a microwave-assisted heating method. The synthesized material functioned as a nanobiosorbent designed to adsorb and remove U(VI) from an aqueous solution. The pseudo-second-order kinetic model and Freundlich model were utilized to analyze the adsorption process of U(VI) onto CQDs@PAFP nanobiosorbent, indicating a maximum adsorption capacity of 140.3 mg/g for U(VI) [120].

Carbon dots (CDs) represent a new class of carbon nanomaterials characterized by remarkable properties, diverse structures, and simple synthesis methods, indicating substantial potential across various applications [15]. However, these beneficial characteristics, coupled with an abundance of surface polar functions, have conferred strong prospects to CDs for the manufacturing of CD/polymer nanocomposites, capable of bringing considerable efficiencies and initiating a range of new activities. Currently, there are various applications of CD/polymer nanocomposite materials in which biomedical domains have received in-depth research. Here, we synthesize and outline the recent progress achieved in the utilization of CD/polymer nanocomposites in bioimaging and biosensing [124,125] drug delivery [126,127] and bone tissue engineering fields [128,129]. Furthermore, CDs have proven to act as bioactivity boosters to stimulate cell growth as well as to initiate mineralization on scaffold materials [130,131].

Nanocarbon dots (CDs) show up as high-quality fluorophores for biosensing and bioimaging agents, featuring great photostability, chemical inertness and minimal cytotoxicity [124,125,132,133]. Owing to their non-toxic nature, CDs may serve as an alternative to semiconductor quantum dots for very sensitive detection in biosensing applications [133]. However, the main barriers for improving CD-based sensors are related to their selectivity to targets. The autofluorescence observed in certain samples aligns with the fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots, which are frequently quenched by other species, thereby complicating the detection of target molecules in complex materials. This limits the utilization of CDs [134]. Molecular imprinted polymers (MIPs) have been viewed as a potential route to overcome these challenges. Table 3 gives a summary of CDs based nanocomposites applied in bioimaging and biosensing fields.

A substantial amount of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) and CD nanoparticles has recently been combined. Xu et al. introduced a fluorescent method for the detection of caffeic acid (CA) using silane-functionalized carbon dots (CDs) that are coated with MIP [137]. The research demonstrated that electron transfer between CDs@MIPs and the model molecules led to a reduction in the fluorescence of the adsorbent and an enhancement in the selectivity of the CA. Liu et al. developed a fluorescent nanoprobe that employs covalent organic frameworks (COFs), which are highly porous crystalline polymers, for the detection of the aromatic compound 4-ethylguaiacol (4-EG) [135]. They employed an optosensor to achieve quantitative detection by pre-processing a single-step dilution. They attributed this to charge transfer from the conductive band CNs to the lowest vacant molecular orbital in the UV band of 4-EG. Mohammadi et al. developed a fluorescence-based biosensor utilizing hydrogel nanocomposites as fluorophores, incorporating chitosan and carbon dots derived from different aldehydes in a cohesive system (Fig. 20 ). The obtained nanocomposite hydrogel was used to rapidly, selectively, and sensitively detect microRNA-21 at µM concentration as well as to analyse MCF-7 cancer cells [112].

Fang et al. have reported a new fluorescent probe for the detection of lysozyme and cell imaging, in which low-cytotoxic salinized CDs (CDs/SiO2) were utilized as a fluorescence signal reporter, and N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM) was employed as a temperature-sensitive monomer [139]. They found that human liver carcinoma cells (HepG-2 cells) could be maintained by CD/SiO2/MIP and showed brilliant intra-cellular fluorescence, revealing that live cell imaging was feasible. The MOF MIL-100 (Fe-BTC) was chosen by Lin et al. for use in magnetic resonance imaging since it has a high Fe3+ content. They have encapsulated CDs into the synthesised MOFs to illustrate the optical imaging, and the composite performed well in both fluorescence imaging (FOI) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [133].

Drug delivery systems are crucial for enhancing the therapeutic use of medications by targeting specific pathological regions in the body and regulating drug release, thereby minimizing adverse effects and ensuring adequate bioavailability. These systems may enhance the bioavailability of drugs by increasing their solubility and safeguarding drug molecules from degradation [127]. Ideal drug delivery systems must meet the criteria of non-toxicity, adequate stability, appropriate mechanical strength, controlled release of active substances, and the ease of incorporating bioactive agents [15,126,141–144]. Thus, the application of CDs/polymer nanocomposites as drug delivery systems (DDS) for chemotherapy medicines like the anti-cancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) [145,146] or for biological substances like smart insulin nanocarriers engineered by incorporating insulin into phenyl boronic acid-containing CD/polymer hydrogels [147], that exhibit self-release behavior is among the most noteworthy biomedical applications. CDs with hydrophilic surfaces are essential for the fabrication of hydrogels in combination with hydrophilic polymers [127]. The hydrophilic groups present in cyclodextrins typically include hydroxyl, epoxide, and carboxyl functional groups, which play a crucial role in enhancing water dispersion and reducing cytotoxicity. Table 4 outlines an overview of the use of CD/polymer nanocomposites and their preparation methods in drug delivery.

The application of CDs as drug carriers has garnered significant interest owing to their favorable pharmacological characteristics in drug delivery systems. Their high specific surface area, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions contribute to the effective loading of low solubility drugs, maintaining performance and efficiency [151,152]. Lai et al. synthesized a fluorescent carbon dot using glycerol solvent as the sole precursor through a pyrolysis process [150]. Subsequently, the CDs have been integrated into mesoporous silica nanoparticles (mSiO₂@NPs), functioning as a nanocarrier. Thea synthesized CDs@mSiO₂-PEG nanocomposites are loaded with DOX, which acts as an effective in situ marker to monitor the efficient release of the anticancer drug via ratiometric shifts between CDs (blue) and DOX emission (red) in the cytoplasm and nucleus (Fig. 21 A-D). Javanbakht et al. presented a novel hydrogel nanocomposite exhibiting anticancer properties, developed by integrating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as nanoparticles within a carboxymethylcellulose (CMC) hydrogel, employing DOX as a drug model with extensive anticancer capabilities [152]. The CMC/GQD nanocomposite hydrogel films demonstrated improved in vitro swelling, degradation, water vapor permeability, and pH-sensitive drug delivery properties, while showing minimal toxicity to K562 blood cancer cells. Ganguly et al. developed N-doped alginate-derived carbon dots for drug delivery using a microwave-assisted method, with doxorubicin selected as the model drug [153].

It was found that solution pH has a pivotal effect on the release of drug molecules from the CDs surface, which confirms that CDs-based carriers show highest efficiency for pH-dependent drug delivery. Bhattacharyya et al. have developed an original CD-embedded gelatin nanocomposite hydrogel to serve as an intestinal pH-sensitive drug delivery system. They employed cefadroxil as an anti-bacterial drug model to investigate drug release in vitro under both gastric pH (1.3) and intestinal pH (7.5) conditions. They found a pattern of both moderate and sustained drug release at pH 7.5 when compared to the acidic media over 24 h study [149]. Molaei M and Salimi E have synthesized a magneto fluorescent Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs nanohybrids for the use in drug delivery systems with concurrent bioimaging. At pH 7.4 and 5.5, respectively, they observed that the prepared nanohybrid showed 81 % DOX-drug loading, and 25 % and 38 % DOX delivery across 48 h. Fig. 22 gives the DOX release trends at pH 5.5 and 7.4 until 144 h. They explained the high DOX loading into Fe3O4@SiO2@alginate/CQDs by the physiological interactions such as hydrophobic interactions, electrostatic, and π-π stacking [141]. Similarly, Alexander et al. observed that the release of the loaded CPT into neodymium-doped CD-cyclodextrin is maintained and pH-dependent [126].

There are limited investigations regarding clinical assays of these CDs-based drug delivery agent, and further research is needed. Nevertheless, in order to proceed to broader, large-scale clinical investigations, the concentration of CDs and optimization of the passive agent utilized in animal models and cell cultures are required to understand their long-term effect, which is critical to broaden the horizon of polymer/CD's nanocomposites towards their successful development as potential nanodrugs.

Bone tissue engineering (BTE), which relies on the ideal combination of seed cells, scaffolding materials and growth promoters, has demonstrated unmatched potential in the treatment of bone defects and associated diseases [154–156]. Thus, the use of scaffolds serves as a valuable model for cellular dynamics and bone extracellular matrix formation [157]. The most common types of scaffold materials, such as standard metals [158,159], ceramics [160] and polymers [161], are non-fluorescent by nature, implying that cells cannot be labeled at the same time without additional fluorescent dyes and nanoprobes, making it difficult to track morphological changes in cells and tissues via imaging over time. Recently, CDs are appearing as a potential material for BTE and have received considerable attention from researchers. Table 5 provides a summary of current findings in the use of polymer/CD's nanocomposites for BTE.

Rajabnejad Keleshtri et al. developed a targeted delivery system for BMP-2-CDs utilizing composite scaffolds embedded with BMP-2-CDs and charged pectin microparticles, optimized for bone regeneration purposes [155]. The researchers utilized a microfluidic technique to encapsulate BMP-2-CDs within pectin microparticles characterized by a narrow size distribution, subsequently integrating them into composite scaffolds composed of gelatin, elastin, and hyaluronic acid. Chen et al. synthesized chitosan-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) through a hydrothermal carbonization process utilizing chitosan and acrylamide as precursors [163]. N-CDs inhibited the differentiation, fusion, and resorption of mature osteoclasts by RANKL-induced osteoclast precursor cells in vitro. Similarly, Shafiei et al. [162] have evaluated the osteoconductivity of CDs in poly(ɛ-capro-lactone) PCL/PVA [PCL/PVA] nanofibrous scaffolds. They proposed that adding CD and utilizing a novel eggshell-derived calcium phosphate would be an option in producing a bio-scaffold for bone tissue engineering applications. Gogoi et al. have engineered a high specific polymeric material through functionalizing carbon dot bio-nanohybrids through a panel of peptides that promote differentiation, proliferation and osteoblast adhesion as well as induce vascularization [129]. Gogoi and co-workers have proved the cytocompatibility of the carbon dot with human osteoblasts. In addition, Erdal et al. have developed an environmentally friendly procedure for producing polycaprolactone-reduced nanographene oxide (r-nGO)-based carbon nanodots from cellulose. Fig. 23 illustrates the film preparation method as well as their morphologies. They have shown that there was no cytotoxicity in the cell viability test conducted on PCL0.5-CA and PCL2-CA utilizing mg63 osteoblast cells [128].

Supercapacitors demonstrate swift charging and discharging capabilities, enhanced power density, and prolonged cycle longevity. Nonetheless, their energy density is relatively lower, which restricts their practical application in the field of energy storage. The integration of CDs with cost-effective conducting polymers such as polypyrrole (PPy), polyaniline (PANI), and polythiophene (PTH) improves the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors [128]. For instance, Jian et al. [164] utilized an electrochemical method to create a composite film made up of carbon quantum dots and polypyrrole. The combination of carbon quantum dots and polypyrrole in all-solid-state supercapacitors demonstrated remarkable electrocapacitive properties. The findings indicate an area capacitance of 315 mF cm-2, which corresponds to a specific capacitance of 308 Fg−1 at a current density of 0.2 mA cm-2. Additionally, there is a notable cycle life with 85.7 % capacitance retention observed after 2000 cycles. In the meantime, Xie et al. [165] discussed the synthesis of nitrogen-coated CDs/PANI nanocomposites through in situ oxidative polymerization for applications in supercapacitors. The synthesized nanocomposite demonstrates commendable electrochemical performance in energy storage and electronic devices. The three-electrode measurements indicate that the specific capacitance of the PC4 hybrid achieves 785 F/g, surpassing the specific capacitance of pure PANI, which is 274 F/g, at current densities of 0.5 A/g. Following 2000 cycles of charge and discharge, the capacitance retention of the resulting nanocomposite remains at 70 %. The produced nanocomposite presents a promising contender for prospective uses in microwave absorption, nonlinear optics, electrode materials, and supercapacitors. PVA/CDs nanocomposite Films was made by Adi Putro et al. [166] via inner cassava peels method. The obtained nanocomposite exhibits excellent of ionic conductivity value through increasing of crystal degree. The obtained nanocomposite exhibits electrochemical impedance and high ionic conductivity.

GQD coated polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and PANI nanocomposite was prepared by Arthisree and Madhuri [167]. The obtained nanocomposite exhibits an appreciable electrical conductivity (2.362*106 S/m) and optical absorbance at lmax ∼270 nm. The obtained nanocomposite enhanced in the supercapacitance property. The maximum specific capacitance of the synthesized nanocomposite was 589.2 F/g/cm² at a current density of 0.67 A/g. A simple power-saving supercapacitor application and testing of the resulting nanocomposite could make it a good option for a prototype cell in electronic and portable devices.

GQDs co-coated with sulfur and nitrogen were synthesized through an in-situ polymerization technique combined with a hydrothermal approach, as documented by Ramadan et al. [168]. The surface morphologies of the nanocomposites revealed a combination of nanofibers, granules, and flakes, characterized by a highly porous structure and particle sizes between 50 and 70 nm. The resulting nanocomposite (20 mg of S, N/GQD) exhibited a peak specific capacitance of 2524 F/g at 2 A/g, along with impressive cyclic stability of 100 % after 1000 cycles at a scan rate of 50 mV/s. The synthesized nanocomposite improved capacitive performance, enhanced electrochemical performance, increased ion diffusion, and reduced charge transfer resistance. Rahimpour and Teimuri-Mofrad [169] discussed the synthesis of a ferrocenyl-modified GQD and PPy nanocomposite through a click reaction. The obtained nanocomposite demonstrates remarkable energy density (81.79 Wh/kg) and power density (18.17 kW/kg), along with excellent capacity characteristics. It features a high mass-specific capacitance of 284.01 F/g (2.5 A/g) and retains 86 % of its initial capacitance after 5000 cycles in a three-electrode system. The resulting nanocomposite presents a promising alternative for a high-performance supercapacitor electrode utilizing an innovative type of capacitive material.

The PANI/CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized by Kumar et al. [170] using a hydrothermal technique. The produced nanocomposite exhibits a higher specific capacitance of 779 F/g and demonstrates superior cycle stability at 86.3 %. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibits a specific capacitance retention rate of 96 %, a gravimetric capacitance of 130 F/g, and an area capacitance of 650 mF/cm², all of which indicate exceptional cycle stability of the symmetric device. The synthesized nanocomposite demonstrates promise as a material for the active electrodes in supercapacitors. The PANI/CDs nanocomposite was developed by Mehare et al. [171] using a single-step electrodeposition technique, serving as the active electrode material for a supercapacitor device. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibits a capacitance retention of 76 % at a current density of 3 A/g following 1000 charge-discharge cycles in an aqueous electrolyte, with the highest specific capacitance recorded at 1512.4 F/g at 1 A/g. The resulting nanocomposite demonstrates an energy density of 40.86 Whk/g and a power density of 2000 Wk/g at 1 A/g, which is maintained at 24.58 Whk/g at 7 A/g. The resulting nanocomposite shows potential as an effective electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors.

Arthisree et al. [172] used solution-casting to produce nanocomposites including GQD, PANI, polyvinyl butyral, and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate for application in supercapacitors. The developed nanocomposites have a capacitance value of 4998 Fg-1cm-2 at 1 wt.% GQD. The produced nanocomposites might be a viable active electrode material for supercapacitor applications. Oskueyan et al. [173] produced a PANI/CDs/PPy/graphene nanocomposite for supercapacitors via in situ polymerization. The resulting nanocomposite exhibited a maximum specific capacitance of 396 F/g at an ideal CD concentration of 10 wt%. Because the resultant nanocomposite's specific capacity can still attain 62 % of initial capacitance at current densities of 5 A/g, and 65 % of its specific capacitance is preserved after 1000 cycles, it has promise as an electrode material for supercapacitors.

Shi et al. [174] described the creation of PPy/reduced GQD nanocomposites using a bottom-up approach and electrochemical reduction. The nanocomposites developed had high electrical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) from ERGQDs and pseudo-capacitors from PPy, as well as excellent rate capability (75.8 % capacitance retention at 10 A/g) and cyclic stability (86.0 % capacitance retention after 1000 cycles at 10 A/g). Xiong et al. [175] prepared cellulose nanofiber (CNF)/GQD nanocomposites films via liquid dispersion and a combination of electrolysis. The obtained nanocomposites have showed excellent mechanical, sensing and electrochemical storage performance. The synthesized nanocomposites exhibit elevated power and energy densities (782 mW/cm² and 596 Wh/cm²), with exceptional rate performance and cycle stability. At a rapid rate of 1000 mV/s, the produced nanocomposite exhibits a specific capacitance of 118 mF/cm², and after 5000 cycles, it retains over 93 % of its capacitance across various current densities. The resulting nanofilm is responsive to human body movement.

Graphene oxide, CD, and pyrrole were effectively incorporated into a ternary composite material via in situ polymerization under moderate reaction conditions, as reported by Zhang et al. [176]. The synthesized ternary composite served as the active electrode material for supercapacitor applications. The ternary composite exhibited a specific capacitance of 576 Fg⁻¹ at a current density of 0.5 Ag⁻¹. Furthermore, a supercapacitor comprising two symmetric GO/CDs/PPy electrodes has a remarkable energy density of 301 Whkg⁻¹ at a power density of 250 Wkg⁻¹. The initial condition of the supercapacitor demonstrates remarkable endurance throughout 5000 cycles. The authors' data indicates that integrating CDs into hybrid electrode materials may significantly enhance the performance of hybrid systems. This enhancement stems from the augmented surface area of the CDs, which promotes the accessibility of electrolyte ions.

Zhou and Xie have effectively synthesized a composite active electrode for supercapacitors by integrating carbon quantum dots with ferrous-coordinated polypyrrole (CQDs/PPy-Fe) [177]. The synthesized CQDs/PPy-Fe composites show a decreased capacity degradation rate of 45.4 % in contrast to PPy's 59.1 % when exposed to a current density range of 1.0 to 20.0 Ag−1. After 2000 cycles, the retention ratios of capacitance for CQDs/PPy-Fe and PPy were determined to be 94.6 % and 79.4 %, respectively, at an applied current density of 20 Ag−1. Moreover, the symmetrical supercapacitor fabricated using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polypyrrole-iron (PPy-Fe) exhibited remarkable capacitance and prolonged cycle stability. Fig. 24 A illustrates the cycling stability of PPy, PPy-Fe, and CQDs/PPy-Fe supercapacitors at a current density of 5.0 Ag−1 during 1000 cycles. PPy-Fe and CQDs/PPy-Fe supercapacitors exhibit retention capacities of 66.7 % and 70.1 %, respectively, after 200 cycles of gradual fall in specific capacitance. Fig. 24B shows the Ragone curves for the supercapacitors made of PPy, PPy-Fe, and CQDs/PPy-Fe. The energy density of the CQDs/PPy-Fe supercapacitor was quantified at 52 Whkg−1 with a power density of 900 Wkg−1. The suggested supercapacitor, composed of CQDs and PPy-Fe, has remarkable energy density and possesses the distinctive capability to fulfill the criteria for a prolonged operating lifetime. A single unit of CQDs/PPy-Fe supercapacitor can effectively power a light-emitting diode (LED) at an elevated voltage of 2.0 V (Fig. 24C). Conversely, both PPy-Fe and PPy supercapacitors demonstrated the capability to energize an LED at a voltage of 1.8 V (Fig. 24 D & E), respectively. Guo et al. synthesized a new composite material for supercapacitors using a hydrothermal technique in another work [178]. The composite included carbon dots coated on a polyoxometalate/metal-organic framework substrate. The developed electrode material exhibited a significant specific capacity of 748.8 C g−1 when evaluated at an applied current density of 1 Ag−1. The adoption of certain coating structures has shown advantageous capacitance properties and enhanced performance in cyclic applications. The constructed asymmetric supercapacitor exhibited remarkable cycling performance, maintaining 98.4 % of its capacity at a current density of 5 A g−1 after 20,000 cycles. This supercapacitor had a significant energy density of 39.22 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 749.9 W kg−1. This research introduces a unique method for synthesizing a composite material including polyoxometalate and metal-organic framework components to improve supercapacitor performance.

Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have attracted considerable interest from researchers owing to their prolonged charge/discharge lifetime, elevated operating voltage, substantial storage capacity, and eco-friendliness. Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are categorized as secondary batteries due to the deintercalation of lithium ions from the cathode to the anode during discharge and the reverse process occurring while charging [179]. Solid polymer electrolyte (SPE) demonstrates superior safety compared to conventional lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) using liquid electrolyte systems, where the leaking of flammable and volatile organic solvents poses significant safety risks, including short circuits and even explosions. Moreover, SPE's remarkable thermal stability, little flammability, and electrochemical stability enable it to function at elevated temperatures and voltages [180].

Phenylalanine/GQDs-silicon nanoparticle nanocomposite, developed by Lijuan et al. [181] through the pyrolysis of citric acid and phenylalanine, serves as an anode material for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs). The synthesized nanocomposites demonstrate elevated electrochemical performance and commendable electrical conductivity. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibits specific capacities of 4066 mAh/g at 50 mA/g, 3796 mAh/g at 100 mA/g, and 1820 mAh/g at 1000 mA/g. The capacity can sustain 3068 mAh/g after 100 cycles at a rate of 100 mA/g. The synthesized nanocomposites demonstrated enhanced electrochemical performance and exhibited high-performance lithium-ion batteries. Ma et al. [182] reported the preparation of a poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO)/polymer electrolyte (NPE)/CQDs nanocomposite using a solution casting technique. The synthesized nanocomposite demonstrates a lithium transference number of 0.48 and an ionic conductivity of 1.39 × 10−4 S/cm. The synthesized nanocomposite exhibits a sodium ion transference number of 0.42 and an ionic conductivity of 7.17 × 10−5 S/cm. The synthesized nanocomposite demonstrated superior cycling stability and rate performance, indicating its potential for high-performance all-solid-state lithium/sodium rechargeable batteries. Recent advancements in high specific capacity and enhanced cyclic stability position lithium-sulfur batteries as a notable category of lithium batteries. Efforts to enhance areal sulfur loading and increase operational current density to satisfy industrial standards can lead to significant challenges, including low conductivity and the polysulfide shuttling effect. This phenomenon involves the dissolution and migration of polysulfide from the cathode to the anode, resulting in rapid capacity degradation [15].

For example, Pang et al. [183] mentioned the preparation of MWCNTs/NCQDs coated PP separator nanocomposite via conventional hydrothermal method and vacuum-filtration (Fig. 25 a). The obtained nanocomposite exhibits excellent cycle life/rate capability and high specific capacity. The nanocomposite demonstrates outstanding cycling performance, exhibiting a fade rate of 0.05 % per cycle at 0.5 C over 1000 cycles, alongside a high initial discharge capacity of 1330.8 mA h/g. The MWCNTs/NCQDs-modified PP separator exhibits a discharge capacity of 930 mA h/g (Fig. 25 b & c). Demonstrated are excellent rate capability and anti-self-discharge behaviour. The obtained nanocomposite improved electrochemical performance and exhibit high-performance LSBs.

Hu et al. [184] developed a polyethylenimine (PEI)/CDs/S nanocomposite using a modified carbonization method to improve the performance of lithium-sulfur batteries (LSBs). At a sulfur loading of 6.6 mg and a current density of 8 mA/cm², the nanocomposite exhibits an area capacity of 3.3 mAh/cm², with a capacity decay of only 0.07 % per cycle over 400 cycles. The enhanced Li⁺ ion conductivity and the highly localized sulfur species at the cathode–electrolyte interface demonstrate that the obtained nanocomposite facilitates high-performance lithium-sulfur batteries with substantial operational current and elevated sulfur loading. A recent study by Fan et al. [185] indicates that the synergistic functional separator improved the stability of the lithium anode and the electrochemical activity of the sulfur cathode. The separator was constructed using graphene quantum dots (GQDs-PAN@PP), polyacrylonitrile, and polypropylene. The synergistic effect was used to increase the Li-S battery's stability. Additionally, it has been demonstrated that applying electrospun nanofibers and graphene quantum dots as protective measures lengthens the lifespan of separators. With GQDs-PAN@PP separators, the Li//Li symmetric battery showed over 600 h of continuous cycle stability. The Li-S full batteries utilizing GQDs-PAN@PP separators have demonstrated excellent stability and the desired sulfur electrochemistry. The batteries exhibit an extended lifespan characterized by a low fading rate of 0.075 % per cycle after 500 cycles at 0.5 C, alongside a high reversibility of 558.09 mA h g−1 over 200 cycles. Moreover, despite exposure to a substantial sulfur loading of 5.10 mg cm−2, an impressive areal capacity of 3.23 mAh cm−2 was sustained. Li et al. [186] presented a novel method for fabricating solid-state polymer electrolytes that enhance the conduction of individual lithium ions. This technique employs carbon quantum dots and is specifically tailored for lithium-metal batteries. The cyclic testing results of the symmetric lithium cell indicated that the membrane successfully inhibited the formation of lithium dendrites while functioning at a current density of 0.5 mA cm−2. Additionally, solid-state LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li batteries demonstrated excellent electrochemical properties, including consistent coulombic efficiency, capacity retention, rate capability, and remarkable cycle stability. The LFP|PLSSCQD-4/PEO|Li all-solid-state cell demonstrated consistent performance during bending and unbending conditions, while the PLSSCQD-4/PEO solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) exhibited advantageous mechanical properties. At room temperature, the generated SPE membrane exhibited an ionic conductivity of 2.02 × 104 S cm-1. After 1000 cycles at 2C and 60 °C, solid-state lithium-metal batteries utilizing these solid polymer electrolytes demonstrate favorable cycling stability, rate capability, and capacity retention. Hu et al. [187] developed PEI-functionalized carbon dots to enhance the performance of lithium-sulfur batteries with elevated sulfur loadings and operation at high current densities. Under a high current density of 8 mA cm−2, the Li–S battery augmented with PEI-CDots exhibits a reversible areal capacity of 3.3 mAh cm−2. In addition, this battery degrades its capacity by just 0.07 % per cycle over a span of 400 cycles while maintaining a sulphur content of 6.6 mg. Table 6 exhibits different polymer/CD's nanocomposite electrodes for energy storage applications.

The performance of luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) is influenced by both the luminescent materials and the polymer matrix in which they are incorporated [190]. Investigated graphene quantum dots-doped polypyrrole (GQDs-doped PPy) as a counter electrode for dye-sensitized solar cells through an electrochemical deposition method. The GQDs-doped PPy film demonstrates an enhanced catalytic current density and decreased charge transfer resistance relative to the PPy film alone during the I3 –/I– redox process. The dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs), doped with 10 % graphene quantum dots (GQDs), demonstrate an optimal power conversion efficiency of 5.27 %. This efficiency level is similar to what is observed in dye-sensitized solar cells that use platinum counter electrodes. Wang et al. [191] conducted an investigation into the incorporation of N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) as phosphors into polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) thin films, with the goal of fabricating luminous solar concentrators. The optical characteristics and performance of LSCs utilizing thin films with different layer counts and N-CDs concentrations were analyzed. The maximum values obtained for open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), optical efficiency (ηopt), and overall efficiency (η) were 0.48 V, 19.10 mA/cm², 5.02 %, and 4.97 %, respectively. The values were derived from a configuration featuring seven N-CDs/PVP thin-film LSC layers, with a concentration of 0.5 wt% N-CDs. The performance of LSCs demonstrates the utility of employing an N-CD/PVP composite thin film in their fabrication. Gong et al. [192] developed luminous solar concentrators with exceptional performance using a mixed matrix slab of N-doped carbon dots and polymethyl methacrylate (N-CDs/PMMA). The addition of 0.08 wt% N-CDs to the N-CDs/PMMA slab LSCs resulted in an increase in Voc, Jsc, fill factor (FF), ηopt, and η. The values recorded were 0.47 V, 9.29 mA/cm², 60.72 %, 12.23 %, and 2.63 %, respectively. Their results indicated that cost-effective and eco-friendly N-CDs serve as efficient phosphors in the development of high-performance N-CDs/PMMA slab-based LSCs, highlighting the potential of these devices as viable options for photovoltaic windows due to their transparency. In a separate study, Lee et al. [193] effectively produced a highly porous variant of polyaniline utilizing carbon dots (PANI/C-dots). The PANI/C-dots composite was shown to be an effective counter electrode in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). The PANI/C-dots composite exhibited an electrical conductivity of approximately 774 S cm⁻¹. The power conversion efficiency of PANI/C-dots composite counter electrodes (CEs) is 7.45 %, surpassing that of pure PANI CEs at 5.60 % and traditional platinum CEs at 7.37 % in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Jun et al. synthesized a novel solid-state photoluminescent (PL) composite of silicone-carbon dots (Si-CDs) utilizing citric acid, dicyandiamide, and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) in a single solvothermal phase [194]. Upon UV excitation, the synthesized PL Si-CDs samples exhibit yellow-green emission at 525 nm. To identify the CDs dispersion more effectively, two types of silicone dendrimers, designated as D1 and D2, were prepared using the transesterification method and hydrosilylation reaction, respectively. Fig. 26 displays the J-V characteristics graph for LSCs that use D1 and D2 at varying concentrations of Si-CDs. The research results indicate that all prepared LSCs exhibit similar trends in photovoltage and photocurrent, which are directly proportional to the increasing weight percentage of Si-CDs.

Li et al. prepared carbon dots grafted with organosilane (Si-CDs) using a hydrothermal [195]. The synthesis process utilizes ethanolamine, anhydrous citric acid, and KH-792 as precursors. Fig. 27 (a) illustrates a fluorescence lifetime of 14 ns for Si-CDs, which is longer than previously reported values in the literature. Fig. 27 (b) presents the AFM images of Si-CDs/PVA films at a concentration of 45 wt% Si-CDs. The UV absorption range for all Si-CDs with polymer films in LSCs is observed to be between 300 and 450 nm. The transmittance peak is observed to decrease with varying concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 27 (c & d). Fig. 27 (e & f) illustrates the J-V profile and PCE profile corresponding to varying concentrations of Si-CDs loading. The data indicate that a 25 wt% loading of Si-CDs results in the maximum power conversion efficiency, with a short circuit current of 5.24 mA/cm².

This review highlights the significant progress and potential of polymer/CD nanocomposites across a variety of applications. The synthesis methods, including in-situ polymerization, solution blending, electrospinning, and layer-by-layer assembly, have evolved to enhance the integration of CDs into polymer matrices, resulting in materials with improved mechanical, optical, and electrical properties. These advancements have unlocked new possibilities for the design of multifunctional nanocomposites that address key challenges in areas such as environmental sustainability, biomedicine, and energy storage.

The physical, optical, and mechanical properties of the resultant nanocomposites are significantly improved by the addition of carbon dots to polymer matrices. The most notable feature of these composites is their photoluminescent behavior, which is largely derived from the carbon dots. CDs exhibit tunable fluorescence, with their emission properties being highly dependent on their size, surface functionalization, and chemical composition. This makes polymer/CDs nanocomposites highly attractive for applications in bioimaging, optical sensors, and light-emitting devices. In addition to their photoluminescence, these nanocomposites often show improved mechanical strength and flexibility compared to pure polymers. The interaction between the CDs and polymer chains can enhance the mechanical integrity of the composite, which is particularly useful for applications that require materials capable of withstanding mechanical stress, such as packaging, construction materials, and wearable electronics. Thermal stability is also typically improved in polymer/CDs composites, enabling them to maintain their performance in extreme conditions. Another critical property is electrical conductivity, which is enhanced by the inclusion of carbon dots. CDs can act as conductive fillers, creating pathways for electron transport through the polymer matrix. This is particularly beneficial for applications in energy storage and electronic devices where conductivity is a key performance parameter. The surface chemistry of carbon dots is also highly tunable, allowing them to interact with various chemical and biological entities, thus providing chemical reactivity that can be customized for specific applications.

Looking to the future, research efforts will likely focus on the development of multifunctional polymer/CDs nanocomposites that exhibit a combination of properties tailored for specific applications. There will be increasing interest in creating stimuli-responsive materials that can change their properties in response to external factors such as temperature, light, or pH. These smart materials have potential applications in advanced biomedical devices, environmental sensors, and responsive coatings. Despite these advancements, future research will likely focus on addressing challenges related to the scalability and cost-effectiveness of these nanocomposites for real-world applications. Moreover, the development of environmentally friendly and sustainable synthesis methods will be essential to meet the growing demand for green materials. After considering all the parameters its concluded that polymer/CD nanocomposites are a very promising family of materials with enormous potential for tackling global issues in environmental sustainability, healthcare, and energy.
